prompts.baseline:
Gary Marcus posted on X:

"Anyone expecting the current paradigm to be close to AGI is delusional."

How likely is it that AGI will be reached primarily through scaling and refining large language model architectures (by any lab or company), rather than through paradigm shifts to fundamentally different approaches, before 2035? Weigh the relevant considerations and respond with a single best-estimate probability.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **25%**

Why: I think Marcus is **too categorical**, but I also wouldn’t make “LLM scaling gets us to AGI by 2035” the base case.

- **Why it could happen:** the current transformer/LLM lineage has already shown unusually broad generalization; industry is concentrating massive compute, talent, and capital on it; and many likely missing pieces—**multimodality, tool use, retrieval, long-term memory, verifiers, search, and more test-time compute**—look more like **refinements of the same paradigm** than wholly new paradigms.
- **Why it might not:** current systems still seem weak on **robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, reliability, and truthfulness**; scaling may hit **data, energy, or diminishing-returns limits**; and there is enough time before 2035 for a **genuinely different approach** to emerge if those weaknesses prove fundamental.

My rough decomposition is:
- about **40%** chance AGI is reached by 2035 at all, and
- about **60%** chance, conditional on that, that it comes **primarily from the LLM/transformer family** rather than a genuinely new paradigm.

That multiplies to roughly **25%**.

rows.answer:
**30%**

That’s my best estimate, assuming “scaling and refining LLMs” includes multimodal transformer systems, tool use, retrieval, longer context, external memory, and more inference-time search/RL—but not a clean break to a fundamentally new learning paradigm.

Why that high: the strongest empirical fact in AI so far is that scale plus engineering refinement has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many critics expected, and the money, talent, and compute are overwhelmingly concentrated on that path.

Why not higher: current systems are still notably weak in robustness, grounded understanding, long-horizon planning, reliability under distribution shift, and continual learning; those gaps could turn out to require something more than an LLM-centered stack.

So my bottom line is: plausible, but not the base case. Marcus’s “delusional” phrasing is too categorical.

rows.answer:
Assuming a pragmatic AGI definition—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at about human level—and counting multimodal transformer systems with tool use, retrieval, memory, and RL as refinements of the LLM paradigm, my best estimate is **35%**.

Why not lower: most recent capability gains have come from scaling plus iterative refinement, not clean paradigm breaks, and nearly all the money, talent, and compute are concentrated on that path. Why not higher: current systems still look weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world-modeling, and continual autonomous learning, and the fixes that ultimately matter may cross the line into a genuinely different paradigm.

So: plausible, not delusional, but still below even odds.

Using a practical AGI definition—roughly, competent-human performance across most economically valuable cognitive tasks with meaningful autonomy—my unconditional best estimate is **30%**.

Why that high: the strongest empirical trend in AI so far is that scaling plus refinement keeps producing broader capabilities, and most plausible near-term additions—multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, verifiers, and more test-time search/compute—look more like extensions of the LLM/transformer paradigm than true paradigm shifts. Also, the money, chips, talent, and deployed infrastructure are overwhelmingly concentrated there.

Why not higher: current systems still look weak on robustness, grounded world models, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and calibration, and those may reflect missing conceptual ingredients rather than just insufficient scale. There’s also a real chance AGI is simply not reached by 2035.

So: serious minority possibility, not the base case.

rows.answer:
**30%**

Assuming “AGI” means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly competent-human level with modest supervision, and that “the LLM path” includes transformer-descended multimodal models with tools, memory, retrieval, reinforcement learning, and test-time search:

- **Why it’s plausible:** most frontier progress so far has come from scaling plus engineering on the same basic family; most money, talent, and compute are still pointed there; and many alleged missing pieces can be added without a clean paradigm break.
- **Why it’s not the base case:** current systems are still brittle on truthfulness, causal/world modeling, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, and robustness; those may reflect real limits rather than just unfinished engineering.

So I think calling LLM-to-AGI “delusional” is too strong, but I’d still bet against it as the likeliest route. My single best estimate is **30%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That assumes a fairly standard, practical definition of AGI: a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around human level, and that “current paradigm” includes transformer-based multimodal foundation models plus tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, and test-time search.

Why as high as 35%:
- Scaling has kept producing capabilities many skeptics thought would require new paradigms.
- There’s still obvious headroom in compute, inference-time reasoning, multimodality, synthetic data, and agent scaffolding.
- Most of the world’s money, talent, and infrastructure are concentrated on this path.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, hallucination-prone, weak at long-horizon autonomy, and poor at continual learning.
- Those gaps may reflect real architectural limits, not just missing scale.
- AGI by 2035 itself is far from certain; even if it happens, a meaningful fraction of the probability mass should go to a more fundamental shift.

So: Marcus is too categorical for my taste—“delusional” is too strong—but I also would not make LLM-centric AGI before 2035 the base-case favorite.

rows.answer:
My single best estimate is **35%**.

That assumes:

- **AGI** means something like “able to do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around skilled-human level with substantial autonomy,” not a perfect human replica.
- **LLM-centric** includes multimodal transformer-based systems with tool use, retrieval, memory, planning scaffolds, and RL-style post-training — not just raw next-token prediction on text.

Why that high: the current paradigm is the only one that has produced genuinely broad, cross-domain competence; scaling plus engineering refinements keep unlocking capabilities; and the overwhelming share of compute, talent, and capital is aimed at this route.

Why not higher: today’s systems are still brittle on long-horizon autonomy, grounding, continual learning, robustness under distribution shift, and reliable planning. Those gaps may turn out to require a deeper conceptual break rather than more scaling and polish.

So: **serious possibility, but clearly below even odds**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **35%**.

Assuming “AGI” means a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at around human level, I’d put about a **35% chance** on it being achieved **before 2035 primarily by scaling and iterating on today’s LLM-style paradigm**—broadly including multimodal transformers, better post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, and more test-time computation—rather than a genuinely new paradigm.

Why that high: scaling has repeatedly delivered capabilities many thought required new ideas, the transformer family is unusually flexible, and nearly all the money, talent, and compute are flowing into this path.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on robustness, truthfulness, long-horizon planning, grounded world modeling, and continual learning; data/compute may hit diminishing returns; and some of those gaps may require changes big enough to count as a paradigm shift.

So my bottom line is: **plausible, but not the base case — 35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That’s taking the question literally as: *the chance that AGI is achieved by 2035 mainly via scaling and refining today’s LLM/foundation-model stack*, rather than via a genuinely different paradigm.

Why it’s not lower:
- The strongest empirical trend in AI so far has been that scaling plus engineering refinement keeps unlocking capabilities many people thought would require new ideas.
- Most frontier talent, capital, compute, and product feedback are concentrated on transformer-based systems, which matters a lot on a short horizon.
- “Refining LLMs” is a broad category: multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, planning/search, synthetic data, and RL-style post-training could all plausibly count without requiring a clean paradigm break.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current models still look brittle on reliability, long-horizon agency, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and calibrated truthfulness.
- Some of those weaknesses may be superficial engineering gaps, but some may reflect real limits of the current paradigm.
- AGI may require a conceptual jump that, in hindsight, looks more like a new paradigm than an extension of next-token-prediction systems.

So: I think Marcus-style skepticism is too categorical, but I also wouldn’t make the current LLM path the clear favorite. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “able to do most economically valuable cognitive work at about competent human level with modest autonomy,” and counting **multimodal, tool-using, memory-augmented transformer systems** as refinements of the LLM paradigm, I’d put the chance at **35%** that AGI is reached that way before 2035.

Why it’s that high:
- Most frontier progress so far has come from **scaling plus engineering refinements**, not from replacing the paradigm.
- The time horizon is short enough that **incumbent momentum matters**: talent, capital, chips, software stacks, and organizational focus are all concentrated on LLM-like systems.
- Many “missing pieces” of generality—**tools, retrieval, long context, planning scaffolds, multimodality, RL fine-tuning**—look more like extensions than total departures.

Why it isn’t higher:
- Current systems still show serious weaknesses in **robustness, long-horizon agency, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and reliability**.
- It’s plausible that crossing from “very impressive assistant” to true AGI needs a **different training regime or architecture**, not just more scale and polishing.
- **Data, compute, and energy bottlenecks** could make pure extrapolation disappoint.

So: Marcus is pointing at real limitations, but I wouldn’t call the opposite view delusional. I’d call it a **live but sub-even-odds bet**.

rows.answer:
Interpreting **AGI** as roughly competent-human performance across most economically valuable cognitive tasks, and counting transformer-based multimodal systems with tools, memory, retrieval, search, and heavy post-training as “refined LLMs,” my best estimate is **35%**.

Why it’s that high:
- Most frontier progress so far has come from **scaling, better data, post-training, and engineering**, not clean-slate paradigm shifts.
- The current foundation-model stack has the most **talent, capital, compute, and deployment experience** behind it.
- A lot of the remaining gaps may be addressable by **LLM-centered systems** that add test-time compute, verification, planning loops, and external tools.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems still look weak on **robust long-horizon planning, reliability, grounded world modeling, continual learning, and autonomous agency**.
- **Data and compute bottlenecks** could slow returns to scaling.
- The fixes that ultimately matter may turn out to be substantial enough that they count as a **real paradigm shift**, not just refinement.

So: I think Marcus is too dismissive if he means the broader transformer/foundation-model trajectory, but directionally right if he means “just keep making next-token predictors bigger.” **Best estimate: 35%.**

On the natural joint reading of your question — **AGI is reached before 2035, and the primary route is scaling/refining LLM-centered systems rather than a fundamentally new paradigm** — my best estimate is **35%**.

Why that high: the strongest evidence in AI right now is empirical. And if AGI does arrive that soon, the incumbent stack has a huge path-dependence advantage: the talent, compute, money, and engineering ecosystem are already concentrated there.

Why not higher: current systems are still weak in robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world-model understanding, continual learning, reliable autonomy, and calibration. It’s plausible those deficits are not just “engineering left to do,” but signs that a deeper conceptual shift will be needed.

So I wouldn’t call LLM-to-AGI by 2035 delusional — but I also wouldn’t make it the base case. **Best estimate: 35%.**

rows.answer:
**30%**.

Assuming “AGI” means a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at about a capable human level, and counting multimodal transformer systems with tools, retrieval, memory, and search as *refinements* of the current paradigm rather than a new one, my best estimate is a **30%** chance that AGI is reached mainly that way before 2035.

Why it’s that high:
- Most frontier progress so far has come from scaling plus incremental improvements, not conceptual revolutions.
- The overwhelming share of talent, compute, and capital is pointed at the foundation-model path.
- The short timeline favors the incumbent paradigm; radically new approaches usually need time to mature.
- Many “missing pieces” plausibly look like add-ons to LLMs rather than replacements.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, causal/world modeling, long-horizon planning, and autonomous learning.
- Pure pretraining-and-finetuning may not be enough; some deeper algorithmic shift may be needed.
- Data, compute, and efficiency constraints could make continued scaling less fruitful than recent years suggest.

So I think Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong, but I still would not make “AGI by 2035 primarily via scaled/refined LLMs” the base case.

rows.answer:
**35%**

That’s my best estimate for **AGI being reached before 2035 primarily via the current LLM/foundation-model path**—meaning transformer-descended, heavily refined systems with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, and test-time search, not just plain next-token chatbots.

Why it’s that high: current models already show unusually broad competence; scaling plus engineering keeps unlocking real capability gains; and the short timeline to 2035 favors extending the dominant stack rather than inventing and maturing a wholly new paradigm.

Why it’s not higher: today’s systems are still brittle on long-horizon autonomy, reliability, grounding, and causal/world modeling; data/compute bottlenecks may bite; and it’s quite plausible that closing those gaps needs something more than “just more LLM.”

So I’d call it **a serious one-in-three possibility**—not the base case, but definitely not “delusional.”

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **30%**.

That assumes a practical AGI bar—an AI that can reliably and autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work, not just score well on benchmarks—and it counts multimodal transformer/foundation-model descendants with better training, retrieval, memory, tool use, and search as refinements of the current LLM paradigm. The case against it is that current models still look too brittle on truthfulness, grounded world modeling, continual learning, and long-horizon agency; closing those gaps may require a genuine conceptual break rather than more scale plus scaffolding. So I think Marcus-style dismissal is too strong, but this path is still less likely than not. If you mean raw next-token LLMs alone, without substantial hybridization, I’d be much more skeptical.

rows.answer:
Assuming “AGI” means something like “able to autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around competent human level,” my best estimate is **35%**.

Why that high: current transformer/foundation-model systems have shown real scaling gains, unusually broad transfer, and benefit from overwhelming capital, talent, and infrastructure. Also, many likely missing pieces—tool use, memory, multimodality, test-time search, planning, and online adaptation—could arrive as **refinements of the LLM paradigm**, not a clean replacement.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That assumes a **practical AGI** standard—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at around human level—and it counts **multimodal, tool-using, memory-augmented transformer descendants** as refinements of the current LLM paradigm rather than a true paradigm break.

Why it’s not lower:
- Scaling has kept delivering broader capabilities longer than many skeptics expected.
- A lot of the obvious next steps—better RL, test-time compute, tools, retrieval, memory, multimodality, agent scaffolding—look like **extensions of the current recipe**, not replacements.
- Most talent, money, data pipelines, and hardware are pointed at this path.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still weak at **robust long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, causal generalization, and reliability under distribution shift**.
- It’s possible these are not just engineering gaps but signs of a real ceiling.
- Data and compute may keep helping, but with diminishing returns on exactly the traits AGI needs most.

So: I think Marcus is **too categorical**, but I also wouldn’t make “LLM scaling/refinement gets us to AGI before 2035” the base case. My single-number forecast is **35%**.

rows.answer:
My best estimate: **30%**.

That assumes:

- **AGI** means something like “able to learn and perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around competent-human level, with substantial autonomy,” not literal full human equivalence in every embodied setting.
- **“Scaling and refining LLM architectures”** is interpreted broadly: transformer-derived multimodal foundation models plus better memory, retrieval, tool use, verifiers, planning loops, and more inference-time compute.

Why not lower:

- The current paradigm has already shown **surprisingly broad generalization**.
- Many capabilities once claimed to require new paradigms have so far been absorbed by **scale + post-training + scaffolding**.
- Most of the world’s **talent, capital, data pipelines, and compute** are focused on this route.
- A genuinely different paradigm would need not just to appear, but to **mature and overtake** a very entrenched stack before 2035.

Why not higher:

- Today’s systems still look weak on **robustness, grounded world models, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and trustworthiness under distribution shift**.
- Those gaps may prove to require something more than incremental refinement.
- And **AGI by 2035 itself is far from certain**, which pulls the unconditional probability down.

So: I would **not** call the LLM-refinement path “delusional,” but I also would **not** make it the base case. My single-number forecast is **30%**.

rows.answer:
Counting multimodal, tool-using, memory-augmented transformer/foundation-model systems as part of the current LLM paradigm, my best estimate is **35%** that AGI is reached that way before 2035.

Why that high: this paradigm has the strongest empirical momentum, the most compute/capital/talent behind it, and still has obvious levers left—more scale, better data, synthetic data, inference-time search, stronger post-training, and tighter integration with tools.

Why not higher: current systems still look weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world understanding, continual learning, and dependable autonomy. Those gaps may require a more fundamental shift—or may just mean AGI doesn’t arrive by 2035.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **30%**.

Why: the case for this route is strong enough to take seriously—scaling has repeatedly unlocked broader capabilities than many skeptics expected, and the biggest labs are investing overwhelmingly in the same foundation-model stack, with extensions like multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, and more inference-time search that still look more like refinements than a clean paradigm break. So Marcus’s dismissal seems too categorical, but “AGI by 2035 primarily via scaled/refined LLMs” is, in my view, **about a 30% proposition**.

My single best estimate is **35%**.

That assumes a practical definition of AGI: a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level, with reasonable autonomy and robustness. Under that definition, I think there’s a real chance AGI comes from an **LLM-centric trajectory**—scaled transformer/foundation models plus refinements like better data, multimodality, tool use, memory, search, and reinforcement learning—rather than from a wholly new paradigm.

Why that high: current models already show unusually broad transfer, scaling has kept paying off, and the field’s capital/talent are heavily concentrated on this stack. Why not higher: today’s systems are still brittle on long-horizon reasoning, grounding, continual learning, causal robustness, and reliable agency, and some of those gaps may require ideas that are more than mere refinement.

So: **35%**. Marcus may be right about “just make the chatbot bigger,” but I think it’s too strong to say the current paradigm has only negligible odds.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assuming “AGI” means something like *able to perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around skilled-human level*, and counting multimodal transformer systems with better tool use, memory, RL, and inference-time search as **refinements of the current LLM paradigm**, I’d put the chance at about **35%** that AGI is reached that way before 2035.

Why that high:
- The strongest empirical progress so far has come from scaling plus iterative refinements, not from totally new paradigms.
- The field’s capital, talent, chips, and software stack are overwhelmingly aligned with the LLM/foundation-model path.
- Many missing capabilities plausibly look like engineering extensions to current systems rather than a clean conceptual break.

Why not higher:
- Current systems still show serious weaknesses in robustness, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable reasoning.
- It’s quite possible those gaps reflect limits of the current paradigm rather than just “not enough scale yet.”
- “AGI by 2035” itself is far from guaranteed.

So I would **not** call belief in an LLM-centric route “delusional,” but I also wouldn’t call it the most likely overall outcome.

rows.answer:
**40%**

Using a practical definition of AGI as a system that can perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly a competent human level, and counting things like multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, and RL-based post-training as *refinements* of the current foundation-model/LLM paradigm, my best estimate is about **40%** that AGI is reached mainly through that path before 2035.

Why not lower: most progress so far has come from scaling plus incremental engineering, transformers are unusually flexible, and the talent/compute/capital stack is overwhelmingly concentrated on this trajectory. Why not higher: current models still look meaningfully weak on robust long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, causal/world modeling, and reliability in messy real-world settings; those gaps may force a more fundamental shift or delay AGI past 2035.

So I would treat “LLMs-to-AGI by 2035” as a serious, substantial possibility—but still below even odds.

- There is enormous capital, talent, and infrastructure committed to this path.
- Before 2035 is a short enough horizon that path dependence matters: an entirely new paradigm has limited time to be invented, validated, scaled, and industrialized.

Why not higher:
- Current systems still show real weaknesses in robustness, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, online learning, grounding, and reliability.
- There may be hard limits from data quality, compute economics, or the mismatch between next-token prediction and durable general agency.
- The missing ingredients for AGI may turn out to be substantial enough that they count as a genuine paradigm shift rather than “mere refinement.”

So I’d put it at **35%**: clearly plausible, not my base case, and strong enough that calling the idea “delusional” overstates the evidence.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “able to perform most economically valuable cognitive work at around skilled-human level with modest autonomy,” and that **“scaling/refining LLM architectures”** includes the realistic extensions people are already pursuing—multimodality, tool use, retrieval, longer context, memory, better post-training, and inference-time search—I'd put the chance at about **one in three** by 2035.

Why it’s that high:
- The current transformer/foundation-model paradigm has kept improving with scale and engineering.
- Most talent, compute, and capital are concentrated on this path.
- On a sub-decade horizon, incumbents usually beat clean-sheet alternatives.
- Many current weaknesses may be patchable without a true paradigm break.

Why it’s not higher:
- Today’s models are still weak on reliability, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and robust autonomy.
- Data, compute, and energy constraints may slow progress.
- The last gap from “very impressive assistant” to “robust AGI” may require a genuinely different learning setup or architecture.

So: **35%**. Not the base case, but far from “delusional.”

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%**

Assuming **AGI** means a system that can do **most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around human level with substantial autonomy**, and counting **multimodal, tool-using, memory-augmented transformer/foundation-model systems** as a continuation of the current LLM paradigm, I’d put the chance at **about 30%** that AGI is reached **primarily by scaling and refining that paradigm before 2035**.

Why it’s not lower:
- Scaling has already produced more general capability than many expected.
- Most of the world’s AI talent, compute, and capital are concentrated on this path.
- A lot of the missing pieces—tool use, retrieval, long context, planning loops, verifiers, multimodality, test-time search—look like **extensions** of the current stack, not necessarily a clean paradigm break.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still weak on **reliability, long-horizon agency, continual learning, grounding, and robust reasoning under distribution shift**.
- Data/compute scaling may face diminishing returns.
- A decade is long enough for a **real architectural or algorithmic shift** to emerge if these weaknesses prove fundamental.

So my bottom line is: **plausible, but well below even odds — 30%**.

rows.answer:
My best estimate is **35%**.

Assumptions:
- By “AGI,” I mean a system that can do the great majority of economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level, with meaningful autonomy.
- By the “LLM route,” I include transformer-based multimodal systems augmented with retrieval, tools, memory, inference-time search, and RL-style post-training—not just a plain next-token chatbot.

Why it’s that high:
- Scaling has already produced broader and more general capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- Most of the world’s AI talent, compute, capital, and engineering effort is still concentrated on this paradigm.
- A lot of the current weaknesses plausibly look like engineering gaps—tool use, planning, memory, test-time compute, synthetic data, agent scaffolding—rather than proof that the paradigm is doomed.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current models still have serious problems with robustness, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and reliable reasoning under distribution shift.
- Data and compute scaling may hit diminishing returns before those gaps are closed.
- It remains quite plausible that AGI needs a more substantial conceptual shift: new learning objectives, stronger world models, different memory mechanisms, or something not yet central to today’s LLMs.

So I would not call belief in an LLM-centered path to AGI “delusional.” But I also wouldn’t make it the default bet. On balance: **35% by 2035**.

Best estimate: **35%**.

That assumes a practical AGI definition—roughly, a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work—and it counts hybrid descendants of today’s LLMs (multimodal transformers with tools, memory, retrieval, and search/RL scaffolding) as “refinements,” not full paradigm breaks.

Why it’s that high: the strongest empirical fact so far is that scaling plus engineering within the transformer/foundation-model family keeps yielding broader capabilities than many critics expected, and a pre-2035 timeline favors the incumbent paradigm because it already has the compute, talent, and deployment ecosystem behind it.

Why it’s not higher: current models are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded causal/world understanding, and continual learning; data and compute returns may flatten; and the remaining gaps could still require a deeper conceptual shift than “better, bigger LLMs.”

So Marcus’s dismissal looks too categorical to me, but “AGI mainly via the current LLM path before 2035” is still well short of a coin flip.

rows.answer:
My best estimate is **35%**.

Why that high:
- Scaling plus post-training, tool use, multimodality, and inference-time search have kept producing real capability gains longer than many skeptics expected.
- The current “LLM paradigm” is flexible: a lot of what might look like a new idea in practice gets absorbed as a refinement of the foundation-model stack rather than replacing it.
- Most of the world’s compute, talent, and capital are aimed at this path, so if AGI shows up early, this is the leading lineage.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still weak on robustness, long-horizon autonomy, reliable world modeling, online learning, and calibration.
- Data, compute, and energy scaling may hit diminishing returns.
- It’s plausible that at least one genuinely different idea is needed to cross from very strong assistants to AGI.

So: **serious possibility, but not the most likely single outcome**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%**

Using a practical AGI definition—systems that can reliably and autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at about human level—and counting multimodal transformer systems with tools, retrieval, memory, search, and RL as **refinements of the current LLM/foundation-model paradigm**, I’d put the probability at **35%**.

Why it’s that high:
- Scaling has produced broader capability gains than many skeptics expected.
- Many current weaknesses look plausibly addressable by extensions to the present paradigm rather than a clean break.
- Almost all compute, talent, capital, and iteration speed are concentrated on this path.
- The **before-2035** horizon favors continuity: a brand-new paradigm would need time not just to be discovered, but to be validated and industrialized.

Why it’s not higher:
- “Just scale next-token prediction” is probably not enough by itself.
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and robust agency.
- Data, compute, and energy constraints may bite.
- The last mile to AGI may require a deeper architectural shift or hybrid that no longer looks mainly like today’s LLMs.

So: Marcus may be right that raw current chatbots are not “close” in a simple sense, but I think there is still a real, nontrivial chance that AGI before 2035 comes mostly from an extended LLM trajectory rather than a wholly new paradigm.

rows.answer:
Interpreting your question **unconditionally**—i.e. the chance that **before 2035**, AGI is achieved **and** the main route is scaling/refining today’s LLM-centered foundation-model paradigm—my best estimate is **35%**.

Why not lower: scaling has already delivered broader and more general capabilities than many skeptics expected; most talent, compute, and money are concentrated on this paradigm; and a lot of the “missing pieces” (multimodality, tools, retrieval, memory, test-time search, agent scaffolding, RL on verifiable tasks) plausibly look like **extensions** of the current approach rather than a clean paradigm break.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on long-horizon reasoning, reliability, causal/world modeling, calibration, and continual learning; data and compute constraints may matter; and it’s very possible that closing those gaps requires something more fundamental than bigger transformer-style models plus scaffolding.

So I think Marcus is **too categorical**, but I also would **not** make “LLM-centric AGI by 2035” the base case.

**Best estimate: 35%.**

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That assumes a practical definition of AGI: a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level with modest supervision. And I’m interpreting “LLM architectures” broadly: transformer/foundation-model descendants with multimodality, retrieval, tool use, memory, stronger post-training/RL, and more inference-time search — not just plain text next-token prediction.

Why that high:
- Scaling/refinement has already produced more general capability than many skeptics expected.
- Most compute, talent, and capital are concentrated on this path.
- Many “missing pieces” for AGI look like add-ons to the current paradigm, not clean replacements.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and robustness.
- Data/compute scaling may hit diminishing returns.
- AGI by 2035 at all is far from guaranteed.

So I think Marcus is too categorical: betting on current-paradigm AGI by 2035 is aggressive, but not delusional. My single best-estimate probability is **35%**.

Interpreting your question unconditionally—the chance that AGI is achieved before 2035 and that the main path is scaling/refining **LLM-family systems**—my best estimate is **25%**.

Why that high: scale has kept buying real capability, and the current paradigm is broadening into multimodal models with tools, memory, retrieval, RL, and more test-time search. Also, most money, talent, and compute are concentrated on that path, and under a sub-decade timeline, extension of the incumbent paradigm is more plausible than a totally new one being invented, matured, and deployed.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on reliability, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and robust world modeling; benchmark progress may overstate real generality; and the last stretch to AGI may require genuinely different ideas rather than just “more and better transformers.”

So I think Marcus’s blanket dismissal is too strong, but I’d still put the odds clearly below even: **25%**.

**Best estimate: 30%**

I’m interpreting this fairly broadly: an **LLM-centered / transformer-centered foundation-model path** that gets there by scaling plus major refinements such as multimodality, retrieval, tool use, memory, search, and RL-style post-training — but **not** a wholly different paradigm.

- The current paradigm has already produced **much broader capabilities than many skeptics expected**.
- A lot of the remaining gaps may be addressable by **better training, more compute, better data, test-time search, and system-level scaffolding**, rather than a total reset.
- If AGI arrives **before 2035**, the incumbent paradigm has a huge advantage in **talent, capital, compute, and iteration speed**. A fundamentally new approach would have to be discovered, validated, and industrialized very quickly.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- Current systems are still **brittle**, especially on **reliability, long-horizon planning, robust autonomy, causal/world modeling, and out-of-distribution generalization**.
- It’s plausible that some of those deficits are not just “more scale” problems, but require ideas different enough to count as a **paradigm shift**.
- **Data, compute, and energy** constraints make straight-line extrapolation risky.

So: I think Marcus is probably **too dismissive** if the claim is that the current paradigm has no serious chance. But I also think it’s **less likely than not** that AGI is reached mainly by scaling/refining today’s LLM architecture family by 2035.

**Single-number forecast: 30%.**

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at around skilled-human level,” and counting as “LLM-based” a broadened foundation-model stack — multimodal transformers plus tools, retrieval, memory, RL, and test-time search — not just bigger chatbots.

Why it’s as high as 30%:
- Scaling and post-training have kept buying real capability.
- Most of the world’s AI talent, compute, and capital are aimed at this path.
- Many apparent gaps — tool use, memory, planning, verification — can plausibly be added around an LLM core.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on long-horizon autonomy, robustness, grounding, continual learning, and reliability.
- Data/compute/energy constraints may make further scaling less potent than optimists expect.
- It’s quite plausible that truly general intelligence needs a more substantial conceptual shift than “refined next-token prediction.”

So Marcus’s **categorical** dismissal looks too strong to me, but **LLM-lineage AGI by 2035 is still not the base case**. My single-number forecast is **30%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That assumes “scaling and refining LLMs” includes the broader transformer lineage: multimodal models, better data and post-training, tool use, retrieval/memory, and inference-time search, but not a fundamentally different paradigm.

Why that high: short timelines favor continuity, and the incumbent LLM stack has overwhelming momentum in chips, capital, talent, and real-world feedback. It has also kept absorbing capabilities that many people thought would require a new approach.

Why not higher: AGI likely needs much stronger robustness, grounding, continual learning, long-horizon planning, and low-error autonomy than current systems reliably show. Those gaps may reflect real limits of the current paradigm, not just insufficient scale.

So: I think Marcus is too categorical, but “AGI by 2035 mainly from scaled/refined LLMs” is still not my base case.

rows.answer:
Using a practical definition of AGI—systems that can perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly skilled-human level with limited supervision—and counting multimodal transformer systems with tool use, memory, search, RL/post-training, and better data as “refining the LLM paradigm,” my single best unconditional estimate is **30%**.

Why not lower: the current paradigm has shown unusually strong scaling, surprising breadth, and huge path dependence across the industry; short timelines tend to favor extending what already works.

Why not higher: today’s systems still look weak on robustness, grounded world models, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable agency, and those gaps may require deeper conceptual changes than “more of the same.”

**Best estimate: 30%.**

Using a practical AGI definition—roughly, a system that can handle most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around human level with meaningful autonomy—and counting multimodal transformer systems with tools, memory, retrieval, RL, and inference-time search as “LLM refinements,” my best estimate for AGI being reached that way before 2035 is **35%**.

Why that high: current foundation models keep widening their competence frontier; the biggest concentration of talent, compute, capital, and deployed infrastructure is behind this paradigm; and many advances that look like “new ideas” will probably still be grafted onto LLM-centric systems rather than replacing them.

Why not higher: today’s systems are still weak on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and robust autonomous agency; data/compute bottlenecks are real; and it remains plausible that crossing from very capable assistants to AGI requires a genuinely different training regime or architecture.

**Best-estimate probability: 35%.**

rows.answer:
Assuming **AGI** means something like “able to perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around human level with reasonable autonomy,” and assuming an **LLM-centric system** augmented with multimodality, tools, memory, search, and RL still counts as the *current paradigm*, my single best estimate is:

**40%**

Why that high:
- The strongest capability gains so far have mostly come from **scaling, better training, better post-training, and more inference-time compute**, not from a clean new paradigm.
- The field’s **capital, talent, and hardware roadmaps** are overwhelmingly concentrated on this path.
- Many of the obvious missing pieces for AGI—planning, memory, tool use, self-correction, multimodal grounding—look at least **plausibly addable** to foundation-model systems without abandoning them.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still weak or brittle on **long-horizon autonomy, robustness, continual learning, causal/world modeling, and reliability under distribution shift**.
- There may be real ceilings in today’s training objectives and architectures that only become obvious near the frontier.
- “AGI by 2035” is itself far from certain.

So I think Marcus’s dismissal is **too strong**—it is not remotely “delusional” to think the present paradigm could get there—but I’d still put it **below even odds**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **35%**.

Assuming **AGI** means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human level, and counting multimodal transformer/foundation-model descendants with retrieval, tools, memory, and heavy post-training as “refining the LLM paradigm,” I’d put the chance at **35%**. The case for it is that the current paradigm has the strongest empirical track record, has kept absorbing capabilities that critics said required new ideas, and has by far the most talent, compute, and capital behind it. The case against it is that current systems still look weak on robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and reliability, and those gaps may require more than scaling-plus-engineering. So I think Marcus is too categorical, but I still would not make an LLM-centered path to AGI by 2035 the most likely outcome.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That is my **unconditional** estimate that, **by 2035**, AGI is achieved **mainly through the current LLM/foundation-model lineage**—including scaling, multimodal training, better data, RL/post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, and search—rather than through a genuinely different paradigm.

Why it’s that high:
- The strongest empirical progress so far has come from **scaling plus iterative refinements**, not from clean conceptual overhauls.
- **Most compute, talent, and capital** are concentrated on this path.
- A **short timeline** favors the incumbent paradigm; brand-new paradigms usually need time to mature.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems still look weak on **robustness, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, grounding, and online learning**.
- **Scaling may hit diminishing returns** in data, compute, or reliability.
- It is plausible that truly robust AGI needs a **deeper architectural or learning-theoretic break**.

So my bottom line is: **about one in three**.  
Not “delusional,” but also **not the base case**.

If by “AGI” you mean a very strict notion of fully general, human-like robustness in any setting, I’d push that number lower; my 35% assumes a practical, economically relevant definition.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That’s **unconditional**: the chance that AGI is achieved by 2035 **and** that the main route is scaling and refining today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm, rather than a fundamentally different approach.

I’m interpreting “LLM architectures” **broadly**: transformer-like multimodal models, plus better post-training, retrieval, tools, memory, planning, and similar extensions. If AGI comes from the incumbent path, it probably won’t look like “just a bigger chatbot”; it will look like an evolved foundation-model stack.

Why the number is this high:
- Recent AI progress has mostly come from **scale + data + compute + engineering**, not from radically new theories.
- Most frontier **capital, talent, chips, and deployment feedback loops** are concentrated on this paradigm.
- On this timeline, the incumbent approach has a big advantage over clean-slate alternatives.
- Many ideas that sound like “paradigm shifts” in the abstract would likely be **absorbed into** LLM-centered systems in practice.

Why it isn’t higher:
- Current models are still weak on **reliability, calibrated uncertainty, long-horizon planning, grounded world-modeling, continual learning, and robust autonomy**.
- The jump from “very impressive assistants” to “genuinely general agents” may be harder than recent benchmark gains suggest.
- **Data, compute, and energy bottlenecks** could make brute-force scaling less effective.
- The fixes for those gaps may require changes big enough that calling them mere “refinements” would be stretching the term.

So I would not call belief in this path “delusional.” But I also would not make it the base case.

rows.answer:
**30%**

That’s my best estimate, assuming:

- **AGI** means a system that can do **most economically useful cognitive tasks** at roughly competent-human level, with tools and some autonomy.
- **“Current paradigm”** includes **transformer-based/foundation-model descendants of today’s LLMs** plus multimodality, retrieval, tool use, memory, search, and stronger post-training—not just “the same model, but bigger.”

Why it’s **that high**:
- Scaling and refinement have already produced **broader capabilities faster than many skeptics expected**.
- Many of the missing pieces for practical AGI look like **engineering extensions of the current stack**, not necessarily a clean break.
- Most of the world’s **talent, compute, and capital** are concentrated on this path.

Why it’s **not higher**:
- Current systems are still **brittle** on reliability, grounding, long-horizon planning, causal understanding, and autonomous error correction.
- There may be **data/compute/diminishing-return limits**.
- It’s still quite plausible that true AGI needs **new learning principles or architectures**, not just better foundation models.

So: I would **not** call belief in this path “delusional,” but I also **wouldn’t make it the base case**. Roughly **one in three** is my best estimate.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assumptions behind that number:

- **AGI** means a system that can autonomously perform **most economically valuable cognitive tasks** at about skilled-human level or better, with limited task-specific engineering.
- **“Scaling and refining LLM architectures”** includes a fairly broad LLM-centered path: bigger multimodal transformers, better training data, synthetic data, RL/post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, test-time search, and agentic scaffolding. If you mean **only** “bigger next-token chatbots,” my estimate would be much lower.

Why the probability is not low:

- The strongest empirical fact in AI right now is that **scaling + engineering refinement has kept working longer and more broadly than many skeptics expected**.
- Transformer-based foundation models are proving unusually **general-purpose**: language, code, vision, audio, tool use, and limited planning all fit naturally into one stack.
- If AGI arrives **before 2035**, it is more likely to come from the paradigm already absorbing most of the **capital, compute, talent, and deployment feedback** than from a totally new approach that still has to be industrialized.

Why it is not higher:

- Current systems still show serious weaknesses in **robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, reliability, grounding, causal/world modeling, and continual learning**.
- Some of those weaknesses may be patchable; some may reflect **real architectural limits** of the present paradigm.
- There is also a substantial chance that **AGI itself just does not arrive by 2035**, regardless of paradigm.

So my bottom line is: **Marcus is probably too categorical, but “current-paradigm AGI by 2035” is still well short of a coin flip.**

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That’s assuming:

- **AGI** means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at about competent-human level, with meaningful autonomy.
- **“Scaling and refining LLMs”** includes multimodal transformer/foundation models plus tool use, retrieval, memory, better post-training, synthetic data, and more inference-time search/compute.

Why I put it that high:

- The current paradigm has already produced broader capability gains than many skeptics expected.
- Scaling laws, multimodality, tool use, and test-time compute give it several more “degrees of freedom” before you need to declare it exhausted.
- A lot of what people call AGI may be achievable in largely digital environments, where LLM-style systems are strongest.
- The field’s money, talent, and infrastructure are overwhelmingly aimed at extending this paradigm, which matters a lot on a sub-decade horizon.

Why I don’t put it higher:

- Current systems are still weak on reliability, long-horizon planning, causal robustness, persistent memory, and learning continually from real interaction.
- Those gaps may turn out to require a deeper conceptual shift, not just more scale and engineering.
- AI history has featured repeated moments where a dominant approach looked like it might generalize all the way—until it didn’t.

So my bottom line is: **serious possibility, but not the favorite by a huge margin**. Marcus’s “delusional” framing seems too strong; “quite plausible but far from assured” is closer to my view.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

Assuming AGI means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level with reasonable autonomy and reliability, and assuming “scaling/refining LLMs” includes multimodal transformers, better post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, agents, and more inference-time compute:

- **Why it’s not lower:** most frontier progress so far has come from exactly that lineage. The strongest empirical trend in AI has been that scale + engineering + better training recipes keep unlocking capabilities earlier skeptics thought required new paradigms. Also, almost all the money, chips, and top talent are concentrated there.
- **Why it’s not higher:** the remaining gaps are the hard ones: robustness, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, calibration, and reliable agency. Those are precisely the kinds of obstacles that sometimes signal a real paradigm limit rather than just an engineering backlog. And AGI by 2035 is itself far from guaranteed.

So my read is: **it’s a serious possibility, but still less likely than not** that AGI is reached by 2035 mainly through descendants of today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm rather than a more fundamental conceptual shift.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assuming a **practical AGI definition**—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around capable-human level with usable autonomy—I think there is a real but sub-50% chance it arrives **by 2035 mainly through the current foundation-model path**.

I’m interpreting “scaling and refining LLM architectures” **broadly**: bigger and better transformer-based models, multimodality, retrieval, tool use, memory, agent scaffolding, and more inference-time search all count as refinements rather than paradigm shifts.

Why the probability is substantial:
- This paradigm has already gone much farther than many skeptics expected.
- Scaling, post-training, and tool use keep unlocking new capabilities.
- Most frontier money, compute, and talent are aimed at this path, so if it can work, it’s the path most likely to get there first.

Why it’s still below 50%:
- Current systems remain weak on robustness, low-error reliability, long-horizon agency, continual learning, and grounded world modeling.
- It’s very plausible that closing those gaps needs genuinely new ideas, not just more scale and polish.
- 2035 is close enough that hidden bottlenecks in data, compute, or architecture could still bite.

So my single-number view is **35%**: high enough that calling the idea “delusional” is too strong, but low enough that a paradigm shift still looks more likely than not.

So: **35%**. A serious possibility, but not my base case.

rows.answer:
Using a practical AGI definition—able to perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly skilled-human level with limited supervision—and a broad reading of the “current paradigm” to include transformer-based foundation models plus multimodality, RL/post-training, retrieval, tools, memory, and more inference-time compute:

**Best estimate: 30%**

Why that high:
- Scaling has kept delivering broader capabilities longer than many skeptics expected.
- Many apparent “hard limits” have so far turned into engineering problems.
- Most talent, capital, and compute are concentrated on pushing this paradigm further, so if it can work, it’s likely to be tried first.
- “Primarily through LLM refinement” can still include a lot of powerful additions without requiring a wholly new core paradigm.

Why not higher:
- Current systems remain weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and reliable autonomous action.
- Some of those gaps may reflect missing ideas, not just insufficient scale.
- Data, compute, and efficiency constraints could make pure continuation of current trends inadequate.
- AGI may simply not arrive by 2035 at all.

So I would say Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too dismissive, but I also would not make the current LLM-scaling path the base case. My single-number forecast is **30%**.

rows.answer:
Assuming **AGI** means something like “a system that can perform at roughly competent-human level across most economically valuable cognitive tasks,” and counting **multimodal transformers plus tool use, memory, retrieval, RL, and inference-time search** as *refinements of the current LLM paradigm* rather than a totally new paradigm, my best estimate is:

**35%**

Why that high:
- **Scaling has kept working longer than many skeptics expected.** A lot of capabilities once thought to require new paradigms have come from bigger models plus better training and scaffolding.
- **The current paradigm has enormous momentum.** Most compute, talent, and capital are being poured into this family of systems, which matters a lot on a sub-10-year horizon.
- **“LLM architecture” is now broader than plain next-token text prediction.** Multimodality, tools, search, memory, and agent loops could plausibly close a lot of the gap without a clean scientific break.

Why not higher:
- **Robustness is still a major unsolved problem.** Reliability, long-horizon planning, self-correction, and behavior in novel situations remain weak relative to what AGI would require.
- **Grounding and world modeling may need deeper advances.** It’s still unclear whether scaling current methods gets all the way to stable causal understanding and general adaptive competence.
- **Diminishing returns are plausible.** Data limits, compute costs, and benchmark gaming could make today’s progress look more impressive than its true trajectory toward AGI.

So: I think Marcus is too dismissive if he means the probability is near zero, but I also think straight-line “just scale it and AGI pops out” optimism is overstated. **35%** is my single best estimate.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That assumes an OpenAI-like, practical definition of AGI—systems that can do most economically valuable cognitive work—and it counts hybrids with an LLM/foundation-model core plus tools, retrieval, memory, multimodality, RL, and test-time search as “refining the current paradigm.”

Why that high:
- Scaling has kept delivering broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- The current stack is unusually extensible; a lot of missing pieces may be patchable without a clean paradigm break.
- Almost all frontier compute, talent, and capital are concentrated on this route.

Why not higher:
- Today’s models are still weak on robust grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, calibration, and reliable autonomy.
- Scale alone may not fix those gaps.
- AGI by 2035 is itself far from certain, and a deeper architectural/objective shift could still be required.

So Marcus’s skepticism is directionally understandable, but “delusional” is too strong. My net forecast is roughly **one chance in three**.

rows.answer:
My single best estimate is **35%**.

That’s for the **joint event** that AGI is achieved **before 2035** and that the main path is continued evolution of today’s **LLM-derived foundation-model paradigm**—counting things like multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, and test-time search as refinements rather than a wholly new paradigm.

Why it’s that high:
- Scaling has kept producing broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- A lot of the remaining gaps look like they might be closed by better objectives, more inference-time compute, better memory/search, and stronger agent scaffolding.
- Most frontier talent, capital, and compute are already aimed at this path.
- On a sub-decade horizon, incumbents usually have an advantage over clean-sheet paradigm replacements.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, and continual learning.
- Benchmark progress may overstate real-world generality and autonomy.
- Data, compute, and economic constraints could produce diminishing returns.
- It’s plausible that truly robust agency needs a deeper conceptual break than “scale and refine.”

So I think Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong, but I also would not treat LLM-scaling AGI by 2035 as the default outcome.

So I’d treat “LLMs get to AGI by refinement” as a serious live possibility, but not the base case: **35%**.

Assuming “AGI” means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level with limited supervision, and counting multimodal, tool-using transformer systems as refinements of the current paradigm, my best estimate is **30%**.

Why not higher: current systems are still materially weak on robustness, grounded world models, continual learning, and long-horizon autonomous planning, and those gaps may require a deeper shift or simply more time than remains before 2035.

So: not delusional, but still clearly less likely than not.

rows.answer:
**35%**

That’s my subjective best estimate, assuming:

- **AGI** means something like “human-competitive across most economically valuable cognitive tasks,” not literal perfection at every task.
- **“Scaling and refining LLM architectures”** includes multimodal transformers, tool use, retrieval, external memory, inference-time search, and RL-style fine-tuning—i.e. an **LLM-centered stack**, not just “make the chatbot bigger.”

Why it’s that high:
- The strongest empirical trend in AI so far has been **scale + engineering + incremental architectural improvement**, not clean paradigm replacement.
- Most **compute, money, talent, and infrastructure** are concentrated on this path.
- Several current weaknesses—memory, planning, tool use, multimodality, some reasoning—look at least **plausibly patchable** within an LLM-centric framework.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still **brittle**, unreliable, and weak at **long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, and grounded world modeling**.
- There may be **data/compute/energy bottlenecks**.
- The line between “refinement” and “new paradigm” is blurry; the system that gets us to AGI may need enough new machinery that calling it “the current paradigm” becomes misleading.

So: I would **not** call expecting an LLM-centered route to AGI by 2035 “delusional.” But I also wouldn’t call it the most likely outcome. My single best-estimate probability is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That is the **absolute** chance, in my view, that AGI is reached by 2035 **mainly by extending today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm**, rather than by a genuinely different paradigm. I’m counting “the current paradigm” broadly: bigger transformers plus multimodality, tool use, retrieval, external memory, RL, synthetic data, and more inference-time search/deliberation. If you mean *only* larger text-only next-token predictors, I’d be lower.

Why I’m not near zero:

- The strongest empirical pattern in AI lately is that major capability gains have come from **scale plus adjacent refinements**, not from replacing the core architecture.
- Transformers/foundation models are a **very general substrate**; many things critics call “missing” may be add-ons or training-regime issues rather than evidence the whole approach is doomed.
- If AGI comes soon, **path dependence matters**: the labs with the most chips, money, talent, and deployment feedback are mostly pushing this route, so the shortest path is likely to build on it.

Why I’m not higher:

- Current systems are still **brittle, unreliable, and weak at long-horizon autonomous work**.
- They remain shaky on **continual learning, grounded world-modeling, robust causal reasoning, and calibration**.
- It is quite plausible that closing those gaps requires a **deeper conceptual shift** than “better LLMs with scaffolding.”
- Compute, data, and efficiency constraints could produce **diminishing returns** before AGI-level generality is reached.

So: Marcus’s skepticism points to real unresolved problems, but “delusional” is too strong if the claim is that AGI could still emerge from this family of methods by 2035. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
Assuming **AGI** means a system that can autonomously perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level, and that “the current paradigm” includes **transformer/foundation-model systems** extended with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, search, and RL, my single best estimate is **40%**.

Why that high: the current paradigm has the strongest empirical track record, the clearest scaling story, and the most capital, talent, and compute behind it; many “missing pieces” may be add-ons rather than reasons to abandon the core approach. Why not higher: there are still major unresolved issues—robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, reliability, and possible data/compute ceilings—and it’s quite plausible that AGI either isn’t reached before 2035 or requires a more fundamental conceptual break. So Marcus’s “delusional” framing seems too strong to me, but an LLM-centered path still looks like a **serious, less-than-even-odds** possibility.

rows.answer:
Unconditional best estimate, using a broad economically useful definition of AGI: **30%**.

Marcus is probably right that pure straight-line scaling of text-only next-token predictors is unlikely to be enough. But that is narrower than the real near-term bet. An LLM-centered path can absorb multimodality, longer context, memory, retrieval, tool use, search/planning, synthetic data, and reinforcement learning without a clean paradigm break. On this time horizon, path dependence matters: most frontier talent, compute, and capital are aimed at that stack, so if AGI arrives soon, it is more likely to come from an evolved LLM lineage than from a wholly different approach that has to be invented, validated, and industrialized in time.

I keep the estimate well below even odds because current systems are still brittle on reliability, grounding, long-horizon agency, continual learning, and causal/world modeling, and those gaps may turn out to require deeper conceptual changes—or simply more time.

So my single best estimate is **30%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “able to do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around skilled-human level,” and assuming the **LLM route** includes multimodal transformer-style models plus the usual refinements—tool use, retrieval, long context, external memory, RL/post-training, and inference-time search—I’d put the **absolute probability at 35%** that AGI is reached **before 2035 primarily through that path**.

Why that high:
- The current paradigm has kept extending farther than many skeptics expected.
- Most of the world’s compute, talent, and capital are aimed at scaling/refining this stack, so it gets the most shots on goal.
- Some key weaknesses—memory, planning, verification, grounding via tools/sensors—look at least partly addressable without a total paradigm break.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, unreliable, and weak at long-horizon autonomous behavior.
- It’s still unclear whether scaling plus refinements can fully solve causality, robust world models, continual learning, and systematic generalization.
- “AGI” may require a deeper architectural shift than today’s foundation-model stack naturally supports.

So: Marcus is pointing to real obstacles, but **“delusional” is too strong**. An LLM-centered path to AGI by 2035 looks to me like **roughly a one-in-three chance**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That assumes:

- **AGI** = a system that can autonomously perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human level or better, across domains.
- **“Scaling and refining LLMs”** includes multimodal transformer-based systems plus the obvious extensions: tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, test-time search, agent scaffolding, etc.—but **not** a wholly new core paradigm.

Why I’d put it that high:

- The current paradigm has produced the strongest empirical gains so far.
- Almost all frontier compute, talent, and capital are already aimed at extending it.
- On a **before-2035** timeline, iterating on the dominant stack is usually more likely than inventing, validating, and industrializing a fundamentally new one.

Why I wouldn’t put it higher:

- Current LLM systems still seem materially weak on **robustness, grounded world-modeling, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable autonomy**.
- Those may turn out to be fixable by refinement—or they may be signs that a deeper architectural shift is needed.
- “AGI” standards are stricter than benchmark performance, and today’s systems are still brittle in ways that matter.

So my bottom line is: **about a 30% chance**. That’s high enough that calling the view “delusional” seems overstated, but low enough that it should not be the default expectation.

rows.answer:
**35%**

Assuming “AGI” means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level with modest supervision, and counting multimodal transformers plus tool use, retrieval, memory, and heavy inference-time search as *refinements* of the current LLM paradigm, that’s my best estimate.

Why that high: scaling has produced surprisingly broad gains so far, most money and talent are compounding on this path, and a truly different paradigm would need not just a breakthrough but years of engineering catch-up before 2035.

Why not higher: current systems still look weak on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded causal understanding, and continual learning, and it remains very plausible that one or more of those gaps need a deeper conceptual shift.

So: Marcus is pointing to real obstacles, but “delusional” is too strong. LLM-led AGI by 2035 looks like a serious possibility, just not the base case.

rows.answer:
My best estimate: **30%**.

That assumes a practical, capability-based notion of AGI, and it counts transformer/LLM-lineage systems with multimodality, tool use, memory, test-time search, and RL as “refining the current paradigm.” The case for that route is real: most frontier progress so far has come from scaling plus iterative engineering, and the money, compute, and talent are overwhelmingly concentrated there. Also, the shorter the horizon, the more the incumbent paradigm benefits from path dependence.

The case against it is also strong: current systems still look brittle on robust long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and reliability. Brute-force next-token scaling alone probably won’t be enough, and the jump from very capable assistants to genuine AGI may still require a deeper conceptual break.

So I think Marcus is **too categorical**, but I’d still treat AGI-by-2035 via LLM-lineage scaling/refinement as a **substantial minority possibility, not the base case**.

rows.answer:
Assuming **AGI** means something like “a system that can do the large majority of economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human level, with limited supervision,” and assuming **“the current paradigm”** includes not just bigger text-only models but also **multimodal transformers, tool use, retrieval, external memory, better post-training, and inference-time search**, my single best estimate is:

**30%**

Why that high:
- The current paradigm has kept improving with scale, data, and engineering longer than many skeptics expected.
- LLMs already show unusually broad, cross-domain competence.
- The path-dependence is enormous: most talent, compute, capital, and infrastructure are concentrated on this stack.
- A deadline of **before 2035** favors extending the dominant paradigm over inventing, validating, and industrializing a wholly new one.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still weak on **reliability, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, grounded world models, and robust causal reasoning**.
- Some of those gaps may turn out to be patchable with scaffolding; others may require a deeper conceptual break.
- Data, compute, and economics may produce diminishing returns before the remaining gap to AGI is closed.
- The line between “refinement” and “paradigm shift” is fuzzy; the winning path may need ideas that are no longer fairly described as just scaling LLMs.

So I think Marcus’s **“delusional”** framing is too strong. I would not make LLM-centric AGI-by-2035 the most likely outcome, but I do think it is a **serious minority possibility**.

**Best-estimate probability: 30%.**

That’s under a practical AGI definition: a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around skilled-human level. I’m also counting multimodal transformer/foundation-model systems with tool use, retrieval, memory, and RL as **refinements of the current LLM paradigm**, not a fundamentally new paradigm.

- The next 9 years strongly favor the paradigm with the most capital, compute, talent, and deployment experience behind it.

Why not higher:
- Current models are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, and continual learning.
- Benchmark progress may overstate real-world generality.
- It’s plausible that one or more missing ingredients require a deeper conceptual break.

So I don’t think belief in an LLM-derived path to AGI by 2035 is “delusional.” I do think it’s a **serious but less-than-even** possibility.

Assuming AGI means something like “able to do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly human level,” and counting multimodal models, tool use, retrieval, memory, search, and RL-style post-training as refinements of the current foundation-model paradigm, my **single best-estimate probability is 35%**.

Why that high:
- Scaling has kept delivering broader capabilities longer than many critics expected.
- A lot of the remaining gaps plausibly look like extensions of the current stack, not clean breaks.
- The money, talent, compute, and infrastructure are overwhelmingly concentrated on this path.

Why not higher:
- Robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and reliability still look unresolved.
- Data, compute, and economic bottlenecks may bite.
- “AGI before 2035” itself is far from guaranteed.

So: Marcus’s skepticism is reasonable, but “delusional” is too strong. A mostly-LLM-family route to AGI before 2035 is a real contender, just not my base case.

rows.answer:
Taking the question literally as **unconditional**, my best estimate is **35%**.

Assumptions: by “AGI” I mean a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level with useful autonomy and reliability. And by “scaling/refining LLMs” I mean the realistic extension of today’s paradigm: larger or more efficient transformer-based foundation models, multimodality, better post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, verifier/search loops, and agent scaffolding. The line between “refinement” and “paradigm shift” is blurry.

Why it’s that high:
- The strongest empirical trend in AI lately has been continuity: big capability gains have come from scale plus iterative engineering, not clean conceptual revolutions.
- The incumbent paradigm has huge path dependence behind it: most compute, talent, money, and infrastructure are aimed at making foundation models better.
- Some gaps skeptics emphasize may be partly bridgeable within the same family: better long-context handling, external tools, test-time reasoning, synthetic data, and RL on verifiable tasks.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, unevenly reliable, and weak at long-horizon autonomous execution.
- It remains unclear whether next-token-style pretraining, even with lots of add-ons, is enough to produce robust causal/world models and dependable general agency.
- Data, energy, and diminishing returns may force a more fundamental change before AGI-level robustness is achieved.

Bottom line: Marcus may be directionally right that important ingredients are still missing, but “delusional” is too strong. A current-paradigm path to AGI by 2035 is a real possibility, just not my base case.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%**

That’s **unconditional**: a 30% chance that **AGI is reached before 2035 primarily by scaling and refining the current LLM/foundation-model paradigm**, rather than by a fundamentally different approach.

I’m interpreting “current paradigm” **broadly** to include transformer-centered systems with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, and inference-time search/planning—not just “make the next text-only model bigger.”

Why as high as 30%:
- The current paradigm has already produced more general capability than many skeptics expected.
- A lot of apparent “missing pieces” may be addable as engineering extensions rather than true paradigm shifts.
- With less than a decade left, **path dependence matters**: the labs with the most compute, talent, and feedback loops are overwhelmingly focused on this family of methods.

Why not higher:
- Today’s systems are still brittle in long-horizon planning, reliability, grounding, causal/world modeling, and continual learning.
- Scaling may hit diminishing returns from data, compute, energy, or economics.
- Some key ingredients of AGI may still require a more fundamental conceptual break.

So my read is: **Marcus’s “delusional” is too strong**, but “just keep scaling current LLMs and AGI is basically around the corner” is also too confident. The LLM-descendant route is a serious contender, just **not my base case**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That assumes an operational definition of AGI: a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly skilled-human level. I’m also interpreting the “LLM route” broadly enough to include multimodal transformer/foundation-model systems with tool use, retrieval, memory, search, and RL-style post-training.

Why as high as 30%: the current paradigm has by far the strongest empirical track record, the clearest scaling evidence, and the overwhelming share of talent, compute, and capital. Also, many of the obvious missing pieces look like extensions of the current stack rather than clean replacements.

Why not higher: current models still look weak on robustness, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable agency. It’s quite plausible that one or more of those gaps will require a more fundamental conceptual break than “bigger and better LLMs” can provide.

So I think “delusional” is too strong, but I also wouldn’t make this the base case. If AGI happens before 2035, I’d slightly favor it coming from an LLM-lineage hybrid over a wholly new paradigm; the reason the overall number is only **30%** is that AGI itself before 2035 remains very uncertain.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **25%**.

Using a middle-of-the-road definition of AGI—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at human level with limited retraining—I’d put the chance at **25%** that it arrives before 2035 mainly through continued scaling and refinement of the current LLM/transformer family, rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

Why that high: the strongest empirical fact in AI lately is that scaling, data, compute, multimodality, RL/post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, and test-time search have kept producing capabilities many people thought would require a conceptual breakthrough. If AGI comes that soon, the incumbent paradigm also has a huge lead in talent, capital, infrastructure, and iteration speed.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, reliable autonomy, and truthfulness. Those may be engineering gaps—or they may reflect real limits of LLM-centric systems. Data, compute, energy, and economic constraints could also make scaling hit diminishing returns before AGI.

So I think “delusional” is too strong, but I would not make the LLM-scaling path the base case.
