rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That assumes a practical definition of AGI: a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level with useful autonomy. It also assumes a broad reading of “LLM-centric”: transformer-based multimodal models with tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, and inference-time search still count as refinements, not a wholly new paradigm.

Why it could happen that way:
- Scaling has repeatedly produced broader capabilities than many expected.
- Transformer/foundation-model systems are unusually flexible across text, code, images, audio, and action.
- Many obvious gaps may be narrowed by scaffolding, tools, longer context, synthetic data, and search at inference time.
- Short timelines favor the paradigm with the most capital, talent, and deployed infrastructure behind it.

Why I wouldn’t put it higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, unreliable, and weak on long-horizon planning.
- Grounded world models, continual learning, causal reasoning, and robust autonomous agency remain open problems.
- Data and compute may hit diminishing returns.
- It’s plausible that closing the last major gaps requires a real conceptual shift, not just more scale and engineering.

So I think Marcus’s claim is too categorical, but I also wouldn’t make LLM-centric AGI by 2035 the base case.

Best estimate: **30%**.

That assumes “AGI” means something like *systems able to perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around human level with reasonable autonomy*, and that “scaling/refining LLMs” includes an LLM-centered stack with multimodality, tools, memory, retrieval, RL/post-training, and more test-time search—not just a bigger chatbot.

Why I’m not lower: scaling has kept unlocking broader capabilities than many skeptics expected; almost all the money, talent, and compute are flowing into this paradigm; and several missing pieces look like they could be added as extensions rather than requiring a totally new foundation.

Why I’m not higher: current systems still look brittle on long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and reliability under messy real-world conditions; data and compute bottlenecks may bite; and it’s quite plausible that at least one genuinely new learning or architectural idea is needed.

So: nontrivial, but still less likely than not. My single best-estimate probability is **30%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 40%.**

Using a practical AGI definition—able to do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly human level with moderate autonomy—I’d put about a **40% chance** on AGI being achieved by **2035** mainly by extending today’s **LLM/foundation-model paradigm**, rather than by a wholly different paradigm.

Why that high:
- Scaling plus engineering keeps producing real capability gains.
- The same basic stack can absorb multimodality, tool use, memory, retrieval, and inference-time search without a clean paradigm break.
- Most compute, talent, and capital are concentrated on this path.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and grounding.
- Data/compute scaling may face diminishing returns.
- Solving the hardest remaining gaps may require changes big enough to count as a genuine paradigm shift.

So: **substantial, but not more likely than not**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%**.

Interpreting AGI as a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level across new domains, and counting multimodal transformer/foundation-model systems with memory, tool use, search, planning, and RL as **refinements** of the current LLM paradigm, I’d put the chance at about **30%** that AGI is reached that way before 2035.

Why it’s not lower: the current paradigm has a very strong empirical track record. Scaling, better data, better post-training, multimodality, tool use, and test-time compute have repeatedly unlocked capabilities many skeptics expected would require a wholly new approach. Also, most of the world’s frontier talent, capital, and compute are already concentrated on this path; if AGI arrives soon, path dependence favors the paradigm that is already compounding.

Why it’s not higher: current systems still have real weaknesses in robustness, truthfulness, causal/world modeling, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable autonomous action. Some of that may be fixable within the paradigm, but some may reflect genuine limits of next-token-prediction-centered systems. Data quality limits, compute/energy constraints, and safety/deployment frictions also cut against a simple “scale to AGI” story.

So Marcus’s blanket dismissal looks too strong to me, but “just keep scaling chatbots and AGI falls out” also looks too confident. My single best-estimate probability is **30%**.

rows.answer:
My single best estimate is **30%**.

That assumes:

- **AGI** = a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level, with limited supervision.
- **“Current paradigm”** = LLM-centered, transformer-based systems, including multimodality, tool use, retrieval, long context, memory, and RL/search-style post-training — not just “make the chatbot bigger.”

Why it’s not lower:
- Scaling has kept producing capabilities many skeptics expected to require a new paradigm.
- Today’s models already show unusually broad competence across language, coding, multimodal tasks, and tool use.
- On a sub-decade horizon, the incumbent paradigm has enormous advantages in capital, talent, compute, and deployment infrastructure.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, unreliable over long horizons, and weak at continual learning, grounded world-modeling, and robust autonomy.
- Benchmark gains may overstate real-world generality.
- Data, compute, and energy constraints — or a true algorithmic bottleneck — could force a more fundamental shift.

So: Marcus is pointing to real unsolved problems, but “delusional” is too strong. **Best estimate: 30%.**

Why not lower: most frontier progress so far has come from scaling, data, compute, and post-training rather than architectural revolutions; the current LLM paradigm is broad enough to absorb multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, and search-like inference; and no alternative paradigm currently has comparable empirical momentum or industrial backing.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world modeling, and continual learning, and it’s plausible that at least some of those gaps require a deeper conceptual break than “more scale + better engineering.”

So my view is: **LLM-centric AGI before 2035 is a serious minority possibility, but not the base case.**

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Why that high:
- Scaling has already produced surprisingly broad gains in language, code, multimodal understanding, and tool use.
- Most money, talent, compute, and engineering effort are concentrated on the LLM-centric stack, so if AGI comes soon, path dependence matters.
- “Refining” can include retrieval, memory, better planning, inference-time search, multimodality, and RL-based improvements without a true paradigm break.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon autonomy, grounding, causal/world modeling, and continual learning.
- There may be hard limits to just scaling next-token-style systems, especially for robust agency and real-world generalization.
- Eleven years is enough time for at least one major conceptual shift if current methods plateau.

So I would not say it’s “delusional” to think AGI could come mainly from scaled/refined LLMs by 2035—but I also wouldn’t make it the majority bet. My single-number estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “able to do most economically valuable cognitive work at around skilled-human level,” and **“current paradigm”** includes **LLM-centered foundation models plus multimodality, tool use, memory, RL, and inference-time search**, I’d put the chance at **35% before 2035**.

Why that high:
- The current paradigm has the strongest empirical track record so far.
- It has overwhelmingly more talent, capital, compute, and deployment feedback than any alternative.
- A lot of the obvious missing pieces—planning, verification, memory, grounding—may be addable as extensions rather than requiring a clean scientific break.
- If AGI arrives on that timescale, path dependence favors the dominant approach.

Why not higher:
- The biggest remaining gaps may be exactly where LLMs are weakest: robust reasoning, causal/world modeling, long-horizon autonomy, continual learning, and reliability under distribution shift.
- Scaling may keep helping, but not enough; some deeper architectural or algorithmic shift may still be needed.
- “AGI” is often defined more strictly than “very powerful useful AI,” and stricter definitions make the LLM-only-ish route less likely.

So my view is: **Marcus is too dismissive, but Anthropic-style confidence is still far from a slam dunk.** My single-number forecast is **35%**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **30%**.

Why that high: most of the biggest capability jumps so far have come from **scaling + engineering refinements** to the current foundation-model paradigm, and the overwhelming share of money, chips, talent, and iteration speed is still concentrated there. If AGI arrives by 2035, there’s a good chance it comes from an **LLM-centric hybrid** stack—multimodal models plus tools, memory, retrieval, RL, and test-time search—rather than a wholly new paradigm.

Why not higher: current systems still show major weaknesses in **robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, grounding, reliability, and continual learning**. It’s plausible those are not just “more scale” problems, and that a more fundamental conceptual shift will be needed. Also, data, compute, and energy constraints could slow progress.

So my bottom line is: **not delusional, but not the base case either**—about a **30%** chance, using a practical/economic notion of AGI.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 25%.**

Using a practical AGI definition—roughly human-level capability across most cognitive tasks, with meaningful autonomy—I’d put the chance that AGI is reached **before 2035** primarily by **scaling and refining today’s LLM-centered paradigm** at about **25%**.

Why it’s not lower:
- LLMs have shown unusually broad transfer across language, code, vision, and tool use.
- Many likely next steps—multimodality, retrieval, memory, better RL/post-training, planning/search, agent scaffolding—look more like **refinements of the current stack** than clean paradigm breaks.
- Most talent, capital, and compute are pointed at this route, so if AGI comes soon, it will probably come from something transformer-centered.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and robust autonomy.
- Pure scaling may hit diminishing returns, data limits, or economic/energy constraints.
- Historically, AI progress often does require real conceptual shifts when a dominant method starts to plateau.

So: I think Marcus’s statement is **too absolute**—this path is a serious possibility, not “delusional”—but I still see it as **less likely than not** by 2035.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That’s assuming “primarily through scaling and refining LLM architectures” includes a broad **LLM-centered** path: bigger multimodal transformer/foundation models plus better data, RL/post-training, tools, retrieval, memory, and test-time search — but **not** a wholesale shift to a fundamentally different paradigm.

Why I put it that high:
- **Scaling has been the strongest empirical driver** of capability gains so far.
- The same core paradigm has already generalized much farther than many skeptics expected: language, code, vision, speech, tool use, agentic workflows.
- **Path dependence matters**: most frontier talent, capital, and compute are still concentrated on LLM-centered systems, so if AGI arrives soon, this route has a big head start.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- Current systems are still **brittle** on long-horizon autonomy, reliability, grounding, and continual learning.
- It’s plausible that the hardest parts of general intelligence require something more than scaled next-token-style pretraining and its immediate extensions.
- **AGI by 2035 itself is far from assured**, which caps this probability.

So my bottom line is: **plausible, but not the base case**. Marcus’s implied near-zero seems too dismissive; “current paradigm gets there by 2035” looks more like a **roughly one-in-three** possibility.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

I’m interpreting this as the overall chance that AGI is achieved by 2035 mainly via the current transformer/foundation-model path—scaled-up LLMs plus multimodality, better post-training, tool use, memory, retrieval, and inference-time search—rather than via a clean break to something fundamentally different.

Why that probability is substantial:
- The strongest empirical pattern so far is that scale and refinement keep producing broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- The incumbent paradigm has a huge lead in chips, talent, software infrastructure, funding, and deployment experience; on a relatively short horizon, that head start matters a lot.
- Many of the missing ingredients for practical AGI plausibly look like extensions of the current stack, not replacements for it.

Why it isn’t higher:
- Current systems still appear brittle on reliability, grounded world understanding, continual learning, and long-horizon autonomous planning.
- Benchmark performance may overstate real-world generality and robustness.
- Data and compute bottlenecks could mean the remaining gap is not just “more scale plus polish,” but something more conceptually different.

So: Marcus’s skepticism is understandable, but “delusional” is too strong. An LLM-centric route to AGI before 2035 is a real contender, just not my default outcome.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Assumptions: by “AGI” I mean something like *able to do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level with substantial autonomy*; and by the “current paradigm” I include transformer/LLM-centered systems extended with multimodality, tools, retrieval, memory, RL, and inference-time search.

Why that’s a meaningful chance:
- Scaling has delivered broader capability gains than many skeptics expected.
- The current stack is unusually general-purpose across text, code, images, audio, and tool use.
- On a sub-10-year horizon, path dependence matters: most money, compute, and talent are concentrated on this route.
- Many present weaknesses may be fixable by augmenting LLMs rather than replacing them.

Why it’s still below 50%:
- Current models are still brittle, hallucination-prone, and weak on long-horizon planning, continual learning, and robust world modeling.
- Data/compute scaling may hit diminishing returns before crossing an AGI threshold.
- It’s plausible that genuinely general intelligence will require a deeper architectural or learning-paradigm shift.

A compact decomposition is: roughly **~50%** chance of AGI by 2035 at all, and conditional on that, roughly **~70%** chance it comes from an LLM-centric trajectory rather than a fundamentally new paradigm. Net: **35%**.

So: not “delusional,” but clearly far from a slam dunk.

If you mean **raw next-token prediction on static text alone**, I’d put the odds much lower.

So I think Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too dismissive, but I also would not call the LLM-scaling route likely. My single best estimate is **30%**.

rows.answer:
Assuming AGI means roughly human-level capability across most economically valuable cognitive tasks, and treating the “LLM path” as including multimodality, tool use, retrieval, stronger post-training/RL, memory, and agent scaffolding—but not a wholesale replacement of the core architecture—my single best-estimate probability is **35%** before 2035.

Why that high: scaling and refinement have kept delivering broad gains; many current weaknesses may be mitigated by better training, test-time compute, tools, and systems engineering; and most of the world’s AI talent, capital, and compute are concentrated on this path. Why not higher: today’s models are still brittle on long-horizon autonomy, grounded world understanding, continual learning, and reliability; scaling may hit data/compute/economic limits; and some of the fixes that matter most may be substantial enough to count as a real paradigm shift. The boundary between “refinement” and “new paradigm” is also blurry.

So: **35%**. Not the most likely outcome, but far from “delusional.”

**Best estimate: 30%.**

I’m interpreting **AGI** as a system that can do **most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level with moderate autonomy**. Under that definition, I’d put about a **30%** chance on AGI being reached **before 2035 primarily by extending today’s LLM-centered paradigm**—including multimodal transformer-family models, more compute/data, tool use, retrieval, memory, search, and RL-style fine-tuning—rather than by a fundamentally different approach.

- Scaling has repeatedly produced capabilities many skeptics thought required new ideas.
- Most talent, capital, and compute are concentrated on this path.
- On a short timeline, path dependence matters: if AGI comes soon, it’s more likely to come from the dominant paradigm than from a wholly new one.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, unreliable on long-horizon tasks, and weak at grounded world modeling, continual learning, and robust agency.
- Data, compute, and energy bottlenecks could slow returns to scaling.
- The missing pieces may turn out to require a genuinely different learning architecture or training regime.

So: **Marcus’s “delusional” framing seems too categorical**, but **LLM-scaling-to-AGI before 2035 is still less likely than not**.

rows.answer:
Assuming AGI means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level, and counting multimodal transformer systems with tool use, retrieval, external memory, RL, and test-time search as refinements of the current LLM paradigm, my best estimate that AGI is reached this way by any lab before 2035 is **35%**.

Why not lower: scaling has kept yielding meaningful capability gains, LLM-centered systems already generalize more broadly than many critics expected, and most frontier talent, capital, and compute are pointed at this route. Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and robust world modeling, and one or more of those gaps may require a genuine paradigm shift rather than just more scale and engineering. So I think Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong, but I would still treat this as a serious minority path rather than the default.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **30%**

Interpreting “scaling and refining LLM architectures” **broadly**—i.e., transformer/foundation-model systems plus multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL/post-training, and inference-time search—I’d put the chance at about **30% that AGI is reached this way before 2035**.

Why not lower: most capability gains so far have come from exactly that path, the current stack keeps absorbing “missing pieces” without a clean paradigm break, and nearly all the money/compute/talent are pointed at it. If AGI arrives soon, path dependence favors an LLM-centric route.

Why not higher: current systems still look weak on robustness, grounded world models, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable autonomous action. Those gaps may prove to need a more fundamental shift, or just more time than 2035 allows.

So Marcus is probably right that **raw next-token chatbots** are not close to AGI; I think he’s too categorical if the question is the **broader LLM-centric paradigm**. My one-number forecast remains **30%**.

So Marcus-style skepticism is reasonable, but “delusional” is too strong. My single best-estimate probability is **35%**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **35%**.

If by AGI you mean a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at around skilled-human level with reasonable autonomy, then I think the “scaled-and-refined LLM” path is the leading single route, but still far from a lock. The case for it is strong: scaling has produced the biggest empirical gains in AI so far; current models already show broad, cross-domain competence; and many likely missing ingredients—multimodality, tool use, memory, search, verifiers, and RL from interaction—can plausibly be added without a true paradigm break. The case against it is also real: today’s systems remain brittle on reliability, grounded world modeling, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and causal reasoning, and those gaps may turn out to require a more fundamental shift.

So Marcus’s skepticism is understandable, but “delusional” is too strong. My single best estimate for **AGI being achieved primarily via scaling/refining LLM-family systems before 2035 is 35%**.

rows.answer:
Assuming a practical definition of AGI—roughly, a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at about a competent human level—and counting multimodal, tool-using, memory-augmented transformer systems as refinements of the current LLM paradigm, my best estimate is **30%**.

Why that high: the strongest empirical trend in AI so far is that scaling plus engineering around the same basic foundation-model family keeps unlocking capabilities skeptics thought would require a new paradigm, and most of the world’s talent, compute, and capital are still concentrated there. If AGI arrives that soon, the incumbent paradigm has a big time-to-market advantage.

Why not higher: AGI before 2035 is far from certain in the first place, and current systems still look notably weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and reliable autonomous agency. It’s very plausible that closing those gaps will require something that feels like a genuine paradigm shift rather than “just more LLM.” So I wouldn’t call LLM-led AGI by 2035 delusional—but I also wouldn’t make it the base case.

**Unconditional best estimate: 25%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like *roughly human-level capability across most economically valuable cognitive tasks with usable autonomy and reliability*—and counting multimodal transformer/foundation-model systems with tool use, retrieval, memory, search, and RL-style post-training as **refinements of the current LLM paradigm**—I’d put it at about **one in four** that AGI is reached that way before 2035.

Why not higher:
- Current systems still look weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and reliable autonomous action.
- Those gaps may require more than “just scale,” or may simply take longer than 2035.

Why not lower:
- The current paradigm has already improved faster and generalized more broadly than many skeptics expected.
- Most of the world’s compute, talent, and capital are concentrated on this path, so if AGI does arrive that soon, path dependence favors an expanded LLM/foundation-model lineage over a wholly new paradigm.

So my read is: **Marcus is too categorical, but the “scaled/refined LLMs get to AGI by 2035” story is still a meaningful minority outcome, not the base case.**

- **Scaling has worked better than many critics expected.** Capabilities have continued to broaden with scale, data, post-training, and tool use.
- **The field’s momentum is overwhelmingly on this path.** Most compute, talent, and capital are being spent on extending transformer/foundation-model systems.
- **If AGI comes relatively soon, path dependence matters.** By 2035, the winner is more likely to be an evolved version of what already works than a wholly different paradigm invented, validated, and scaled from scratch.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- **Current systems are still brittle** on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and robust autonomy.
- **There may be diminishing returns** from more compute and internet-scale data.
- **The last gap may require something genuinely new** in learning objectives, memory, embodiment, or reasoning architecture.

So my read is: Marcus is **too categorical**, but “AGI by mainly scaling/refining the LLM paradigm before 2035” is **a substantial minority bet, not the base case**.

**35%**

That estimate assumes a practical, capabilities-based notion of AGI, and treats “LLM-based” broadly: scaled multimodal transformer/foundation models plus tool use, retrieval, memory, and RL-style post-training, but not a clean jump to a fundamentally different paradigm.

Why it’s this high:
- The current paradigm has already produced surprisingly broad competence across language, code, vision, and audio.
- Scaling, better training data, inference-time compute, and agent/tool scaffolding are still yielding meaningful gains.
- Most of the world’s talent, compute, and capital are concentrated on this path.
- Many things that look like “new ideas” may end up being absorbed as extensions of the foundation-model stack rather than true paradigm breaks.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems still look weak on robustness, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable causal/world modeling.
- Data, compute, and energy constraints could make further scaling less effective.
- Some of the remaining gaps may require architectural ideas that are more than just refinement.

So my best forecast is that an LLM-centric route to AGI before 2035 is a serious possibility, but not the base case.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That’s using a practical definition of AGI: a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at about skilled-human level, and treating the “current paradigm” broadly enough to include transformer-centered multimodal models plus retrieval, tool use, external memory, verifiers, and RL-style post-training.

Why not lower:
- The last few years have shown repeated capability gains from scale + engineering refinement, including abilities many critics expected would require a new paradigm.
- If AGI arrives **before 2035**, path dependence matters: most talent, compute, and capital are already concentrated on the LLM/foundation-model stack, so a near-term AGI is more likely to come from extending that stack than from inventing and maturing a wholly different one.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still meaningfully weak at robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world modeling, causal reasoning, and continual learning.
- Some of those gaps may be patchable; some may reflect real limits of the current approach. If the latter is true, a paradigm shift will be needed.

So my read is: Marcus is **too categorical**, but “AGI from scaled/refined LLMs by 2035” is still **well below even odds**.

rows.answer:
**30%.**

Using a practical definition of AGI—systems that can do most economically useful cognitive work at roughly human level, with enough autonomy and reliability to be widely deployable—and counting LLM-descended multimodal systems with tool use, memory, search, and reinforcement-learning-style post-training as part of the same broad paradigm, my best estimate is **30%** that AGI is achieved mainly by scaling and refining that paradigm before 2035.

Why not lower: the strongest empirical fact so far is that scale plus iterative engineering has repeatedly produced capabilities that many critics thought would require a new paradigm; the current stack has enormous momentum in talent, capital, hardware, and deployment; and several obvious gaps could plausibly be narrowed by better training, inference-time compute, external tools, and agentic scaffolding rather than a clean conceptual break.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle in reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and robust autonomous operation; some of those weaknesses may be structural rather than just “not enough scale yet”; data, compute, and energy constraints may matter; and AGI before 2035 is itself far from assured.

So: I think Marcus’s dismissal is too strong, but I also would not treat “current LLM paradigm gets us to AGI by 2035” as the base case.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Under a practical definition of AGI—something that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at about a competent human level, with moderate autonomy—I’d put the chance at **35%** that it arrives **before 2035 primarily by extending today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm**, rather than by a clean break to a fundamentally different approach.

Why that high: scaling has already produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected; most plausible “missing pieces” (multimodality, tool use, memory, retrieval, search, better post-training) look more like **refinements of the current stack** than replacements; and almost all the money, talent, and compute are flowing into that path.

Why not higher: current systems are still weak on **robust long-horizon reasoning, reliable autonomy, causal/world modeling, grounding, and error rates you can trust in the wild**. Those gaps may turn out to require deeper algorithmic changes than “more scale + better engineering” can provide.

So: **not delusional, but not the base case either.**

rows.answer:
Assuming “scaling and refining LLMs” includes transformer-derived multimodal systems with better RL, memory, retrieval, tool use, and search—not just bigger plain next-token predictors—my single best estimate is **30%**.

Why that high:
- The current paradigm has been the only one recently delivering broad, compounding gains across many domains.
- Most of the world’s AI talent, compute, and capital are concentrated on this path, and short timelines usually favor the incumbent approach.
- Several current weaknesses may be patchable by extensions that still look more like refinement than revolution.

Why not higher:
- AGI before 2035 is itself far from guaranteed.
- Today’s systems still seem weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and reliable autonomy.
- It’s plausible that closing those gaps will require a real conceptual break, not just more scale plus engineering.

So Marcus may be right that “just scale today’s models” is unlikely to be enough. But I would not call an LLM-lineage route to AGI before 2035 delusional—just clearly less likely than not.

rows.answer:
Based on the evidence through mid-2024, and taking AGI to mean a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly competent-human level with limited supervision, my single best estimate is **35%**.

Why it’s that high: scaling has produced broad, surprising gains; the field’s money, talent, and compute are overwhelmingly concentrated on this path; and many likely missing pieces—multimodality, tool use, memory, retrieval, search, and RL-style fine-tuning—look like extensions of the current transformer/LLM paradigm rather than wholly new paradigms.

Why it’s not higher: current systems still look weak on robust reasoning, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, calibration, and reliability under distribution shift; data/compute/economic limits may bite; and AI history gives plenty of examples where a dominant approach looked like the road to generality until it hit a wall.

So: plausible, and arguably the leading single route, but still less likely than not before 2035.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That assumes a reasonably strict, operational definition of AGI: a system that can autonomously perform most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly skilled-human level, and that “current paradigm” includes transformer/foundation-model systems extended with multimodality, tools, retrieval, memory, inference-time search, and RL-based post-training.

Why I put it that high:
- Most of the biggest capability jumps so far have come from scaling, better data, better post-training, and better scaffolding—not clean-slate new paradigms.
- The overwhelming share of talent, compute, and capital is still aimed at that path, so if AGI appears soon, it is likelier to emerge from the dominant track than from an entirely different one.
- Many of the obvious missing pieces—planning, memory, grounding, tool use, self-correction—look at least plausibly like extensions of the foundation-model paradigm rather than proof it must fail.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- Current systems are still weak on robustness, truthfulness, causal/world modeling, long-horizon autonomy, and continual learning.
- It’s quite plausible that fixing those gaps will require ideas that, in hindsight, look like a real paradigm shift rather than mere scaling/refinement.
- And AGI by 2035 is itself far from assured.

So: Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong, but “scaled-and-refined LLMs get us to AGI before 2035” still looks to me like a substantial minority scenario, not the base case.

**Best estimate: 35%.**

That is the **unconditional** probability that AGI is achieved **before 2035** and that the main route is the **current LLM/foundation-model lineage**—broadly including scaling, better data, multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL/post-training, and inference-time search, but **not** a wholesale shift to a fundamentally new paradigm.

Those may be solvable within the present stack, but they may also require changes big enough to count as a new paradigm, or simply keep AGI out of reach by 2035.

So my view is: **substantial minority chance, but not the most likely outcome — 35%.**

rows.answer:
Assuming AGI means a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly human level, and that “scaling and refining LLMs” includes multimodality, tool use, memory, retrieval, RL, and test-time search, my best estimate is **35%**.

Why that high: the current paradigm has kept delivering broader capabilities than many skeptics expected, capital and talent are overwhelmingly concentrated on it, and several obvious gaps look more like hard engineering problems than proof of a dead end.

Why not higher: today’s systems are still brittle, weak at long-horizon autonomy and grounded world modeling, and it’s plausible that crossing from powerful assistants to AGI will require a deeper conceptual break than “more scale plus better scaffolding.”

So: a serious possibility, but still a minority-chance bet.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Using a practical definition of AGI—systems that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly human level, with tool use and reasonable autonomy—I’d put the chance at **35%** that it is reached **before 2035 primarily by scaling and extending today’s LLM-centered paradigm**, rather than by a fundamentally different one.

Why that’s not lower:
- The strongest empirical fact in AI over the last several years has been that **scale plus refinement keeps working**.
- The same basic family has already expanded from text to code, vision, audio, tool use, and limited agency.
- A lot of the “missing pieces” for AGI—better memory, search, verification, multimodal grounding, test-time reasoning, RL fine-tuning—look like they could be added **without abandoning the paradigm**.
- Most of the world’s compute, talent, and capital are concentrated on this path, which matters a lot on a sub-decade timeline.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems are still **brittle, unreliable, and weak at long-horizon planning and continual learning**.
- It’s plausible that robust general intelligence needs something more than scaled next-token-style modeling: better world models, online learning, causal abstraction, or architectures we don’t yet have.
- Data and compute may show meaningful diminishing returns before the remaining gaps close.
- “AGI” in the real world is harder than looking good on benchmarks.

So: Marcus is, in my view, **too categorical**, but I still think the odds that AGI arrives by **this** route before 2035 are **substantial but below even**.

Using a practical AGI definition—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at around human level—and counting multimodal transformers with tool use, memory, retrieval, and RL as refinements of today’s paradigm, my subjective best estimate is **30%**.

Why not lower: the current LLM/foundation-model stack has the strongest empirical momentum, massive investment behind it, and many likely “missing pieces” could plausibly be added without abandoning the paradigm.

Why not higher: today’s systems are still notably brittle on reliability, grounding, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and long-horizon autonomy, and those gaps may turn out to require a real architectural shift.

So my single-number forecast is **30%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%**

Using a practical definition of AGI—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at about skilled-human level with reasonable autonomy—I’d put the chance at **35%** that it is reached **before 2035 primarily through scaling and refining LLM-centered systems**, rather than via a fundamentally different paradigm.

Why that high: recent progress has come overwhelmingly from the same broad stack, and a lot of things once claimed to require new paradigms have so far looked more like scale, better training, tool use, multimodality, memory, and inference-time search. Also, on a sub-decade horizon, path dependence matters: most of the money, compute, talent, and deployment infrastructure are aimed at transformer/LLM-like systems.

Why not higher: current systems still seem weak on robustness, grounding, long-horizon planning, continual learning, and reliable agency. Those may turn out to need genuinely new core ideas rather than just more scale and scaffolding.

So: Marcus’s blanket dismissal seems too strong to me, but “LLM-centered refinement gets to AGI by 2035” is still well below a coin flip.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

I’m interpreting “scaling and refining LLM architectures” **broadly**: transformer/foundation-model systems that get bigger and better, plus multimodality, retrieval, tools, memory, and inference-time search—not just “same chatbot, more parameters.”

Why that high:
- The current paradigm has shown unusually strong, broad capability gains from scale and engineering.
- It has a massive lead in talent, capital, chips, data pipelines, and real-world iteration.
- If AGI happens **before 2035**, the shortest path is often the one already working, not a wholly new paradigm.

Why not higher:
- Today’s systems still look weak on robust long-horizon planning, grounded causal reasoning, continual learning, and reliability.
- Data and compute bottlenecks may make further gains harder.
- Getting from “very impressive assistants” to “robust AGI” may require at least one conceptual jump that is bigger than mere refinement.

So my bottom line is: **about a one-in-three chance**.  
That makes the LLM-first route **plausible, not delusional**—but still **not the most likely single outcome**.

Assuming AGI means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly skilled-human level, with tool use and modest supervision, my best estimate is **40%** that it is reached **before 2035 primarily by scaling and refining LLM-centric systems**.

Why that high:
- The current paradigm has a strong empirical record: scale, better data, multimodality, RL/post-training, tool use, and inference-time search have kept producing capabilities many people thought would need new ideas.
- “Refining LLMs” is a big design space; it can include memory, retrieval, agents, verifiers, planners, and world-model-like components without abandoning the paradigm.
- Path dependence matters: the talent, compute, and capital are overwhelmingly concentrated on this route.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle, hallucinate, struggle with long-horizon autonomy, and seem weak in grounded causal/world modeling and continual learning.
- Pure scaling is likely not enough; even within the current paradigm, some missing ingredients may turn out to be substantial departures.
- Benchmarks may overstate how close we are to robust real-world generality.

So I think Marcus is **too categorical**, but I also would **not** make LLM-scaling-to-AGI the base case.

**Single best-estimate probability: 40%**.

Interpreting this unconditionally, my best estimate is **35%**.

That assumes “current paradigm” includes transformer-like foundation models plus multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, and stronger post-training/RL—but not a wholesale shift to a fundamentally different learning framework.

Why that high: the incumbent paradigm keeps delivering broad capability gains, short timelines favor the approach with the most capital and talent behind it, and many current gaps look more like engineering/reliability problems than decisive evidence of a hard ceiling.

Why not higher: today’s systems are still weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounded world modeling, and online adaptation; scaling may run into data/compute/economic limits; and the final step to AGI may require changes substantial enough to count as a new paradigm.

So I think Marcus is too categorical, but I also would not bet on plain scaling alone.

rows.answer:
Using a middle-of-the-road definition of AGI—roughly, a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at least at a competent human level—and counting multimodal transformer systems with tool use, retrieval, memory, and search as “refined LLMs,” my best estimate is **35%**.

Why it’s not lower:
- The transformer/LLM family is the only paradigm so far that has shown genuinely broad transfer across language, code, vision, speech, and tool use.
- Scaling has repeatedly produced capabilities that many skeptics expected would require a different paradigm.
- Most of the world’s AI talent, capital, and compute are concentrated on this path, which matters a lot on a sub-decade horizon.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current models are still weak on robust long-horizon planning, grounded world modeling, reliability, and out-of-distribution generalization.
- Data, compute, and energy constraints could make further scaling less fruitful than the last few years suggest.
- The changes needed to close the remaining gap may turn out to be large enough to count as a genuine paradigm shift, not just refinement.

So: Marcus’s blanket dismissal looks too strong to me, but I would not make LLM-centric AGI-before-2035 the base case.

**Best estimate: 35%.**

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **35%**.

That’s my **unconditional** estimate that AGI is reached **before 2035** mainly via an **LLM-centric trajectory**—meaning larger and better foundation models plus things like multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL, and agent scaffolding—rather than via a genuinely different paradigm.

Why it’s not lower: the current paradigm has produced broad, unexpected capability gains; it has enormous momentum in talent, capital, and compute; and many supposed “missing pieces” could plausibly arrive as extensions of the same basic stack rather than as a clean break.

Why it’s not higher: current systems still look shaky on robustness, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and reliable reasoning; there may be real ceilings from data, compute, or architecture; and over a decade, a serious paradigm shift is plenty possible if those weaknesses prove fundamental.

So my bottom line is: **LLM-driven AGI by 2035 is plausible and maybe the leading single path, but still less likely than not.**

rows.answer:
Assuming AGI means roughly human-level performance across most economically valuable cognitive tasks, my best estimate is **35%**.

I’m interpreting “scaling and refining LLMs” broadly: transformer/LLM-centered systems with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL-style post-training, and more test-time compute still count; a wholesale move to a very different core paradigm does not.

Why that high: scaling and refinement have already produced surprisingly broad capabilities, the short timeline favors the incumbent approach that already has most of the money/chips/talent behind it, and many of the missing pieces could plausibly be added without abandoning the LLM core.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on reliability, grounded understanding, long-horizon agency, and continual learning, and those gaps may turn out to require a deeper architectural break than “better, bigger LLMs” can provide.

So I think Marcus is too categorical, but I also wouldn’t make LLM-centric AGI-by-2035 the base case.

rows.answer:
Definitions matter, but if you force a single number, my best estimate is **35%**.

That’s **not** because I think “just make today’s chatbots bigger” is likely to work. It’s because I think the relevant “current paradigm” is broader than that: **transformer-centered foundation models plus multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, RL-style post-training, and inference-time search/planning**. If those still count as refinements rather than a true paradigm shift, then an LLM-centered path is very plausible.

Why the number is that high:
- **Most actual progress since 2019 has come from scaling and polishing this family of methods**, not replacing it.
- **Capital and talent are massively concentrated** on this path, which matters a lot on a 10-year horizon.
- Several supposed missing ingredients for AGI—planning, external memory, verification, tool use, self-improvement loops—look increasingly like **system-level additions to LLMs**, not necessarily a clean break from them.
- The “**bitter lesson**” still points in favor of general-purpose, compute-hungry methods.

Why it isn’t higher:
- Current models still show major weaknesses in **reliability, long-horizon agency, grounded world models, continual learning, and robustness under distribution shift**.
- **Data and compute scaling may hit diminishing returns** before those gaps close.
- It’s quite possible that the last big step to AGI needs a **different training objective, memory architecture, or learning paradigm** that would count as a real conceptual shift.

So: I think Marcus is **too categorical**. Believing AGI could come mainly from scaling/refining the present paradigm is **not delusional**. But I also wouldn’t make it the median outcome.

**Best single-number forecast: 35%.**

rows.answer:
On an unconditional basis, my single best estimate is **25%**.

That assumes AGI means roughly human-level performance across most economically valuable cognitive tasks, and that multimodal, tool-using, memory-augmented transformer systems still count as refinements of today’s LLM paradigm. The case for a higher number is that scaling has kept delivering real capability gains, almost all frontier talent and compute are concentrated on this path, and many “missing pieces” may be add-ons rather than a clean architectural break. The case for a lower number is that current systems still look brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, continual learning, and causal/world modeling, and fixing those gaps may require a deeper paradigm shift. So: a serious minority possibility, not a delusional one.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That assumes a **practical AGI** standard: a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at about human level, and that **“scaling/refining LLMs”** includes multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, better post-training, and inference-time search.

Why I put it that high:
- The current paradigm has already gained surprisingly broad capabilities through scale and refinement, not through clean conceptual revolutions.
- There is still meaningful runway in compute, synthetic data, multimodal training, and test-time reasoning.
- A sub-2035 timeline favors the incumbent stack: most talent, capital, chips, and deployment feedback are concentrated on LLM-centric systems.

Why I don’t put it higher:
- Current models still look brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and low-error autonomy.
- Pretraining on web-scale data may face diminishing returns.
- The last stretch to AGI may require a deeper conceptual shift than can honestly be called “just more scale and polish.”

So my read is: **not delusional, but not the base case either**. If AGI arrives before 2035, I think there’s a substantial chance it comes from an evolved LLM-centric hybrid rather than a wholly new paradigm, but overall I’d still put that scenario at **35%**.

rows.answer:
Using a practical AGI definition—able to do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at roughly human level with modest supervision—and treating the “LLM route” broadly as scaled transformer-based foundation models plus multimodality, tools, retrieval, memory, RL, and more inference-time search, my **unconditional** best estimate is:

**35%**

Why that high: scaling has already produced more capability than many critics expected; the current paradigm still has several obvious improvement levers left; multiple major labs are pursuing it in parallel; and on a before-2035 timeline, the incumbent approach has a big advantage over a truly new paradigm that would need time to be invented, validated, and scaled.

Why not higher: current systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and robust autonomy; data/compute/energy constraints may bite; and it remains quite plausible that real AGI needs a deeper conceptual break than an LLM-centered stack can deliver.

So I would say Marcus’s skepticism is understandable, but “delusional” is too strong. My single best estimate is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

That estimate assumes:

- **AGI** means a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level, with limited supervision.
- **“Scaling and refining LLMs”** includes multimodal transformers, tool use, retrieval, longer context, external memory, RL post-training, and inference-time search/planning layered around an LLM core.

Why it’s not lower: the strongest evidence we have is empirical—most major capability gains so far have come from scaling and engineering the current paradigm, and the money, chips, data pipelines, and top talent are overwhelmingly concentrated there.

Why it’s not higher: current systems still have serious weaknesses in **reliability, long-horizon agency, online learning, grounded world modeling, and rare-failure robustness**. Any one of those could prove to need a more fundamental shift, or simply delay AGI past 2035.

So my view is: **LLM-centric AGI before 2035 is a real possibility, but not the base case.** Marcus’s “delusional” framing seems too strong to me, but so does treating it as likely.

rows.answer:
I’m interpreting “scaling and refining LLM architectures” broadly: transformer/foundation-model systems with multimodality, tool use, retrieval, external memory, RL, and inference-time search still count as the current paradigm.

**25%**

Why that high: recent frontier gains have mostly come from scale plus engineering refinements, the incumbent stack has a huge lead in talent, compute, capital, and deployment experience, and the line between “refinement” and “paradigm shift” is fuzzy enough that many plausible gap-closing additions still look LLM-centric.

Why not higher: AGI on that horizon is itself far from assured, and current systems remain notably weak on robustness, grounded world-modeling, continual learning, long-horizon autonomy, and reliability. Those weaknesses could turn out to require deeper conceptual changes than the current playbook provides.

So I think Marcus is too categorical, but an LLM-centric path to AGI by then still looks like a substantial minority bet, not the base case.

rows.answer:
Assuming **AGI** means something like “human-level or better across most economically valuable cognitive tasks,” and counting **multimodal transformer/foundation-model systems with tools, memory, retrieval, RL, and inference-time search** as refinements of the current LLM paradigm, my **single best estimate is 30%**.

Why not lower:
- The current paradigm has kept absorbing capabilities skeptics said needed a new approach.
- Transformers/foundation models are unusually flexible and can be extended without a clean paradigm break.
- The talent, compute, and capital stack is overwhelmingly pointed at this path, which matters a lot on a sub-10-year horizon.
- AGI may arrive as a messy, tool-using system before we get anything like elegant “human-like understanding.”

Why not higher:
- Today’s systems are still brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, persistent memory, continual learning, and robust real-world grounding.
- Next-token-style training is not obviously the right objective for truth, causality, or autonomous competence.
- Data, compute, and energy scaling may hit diminishing returns.
- Historically, some apparent “last-mile” problems in AI have turned out to require more than scaling and polish.

So: **Marcus’s “delusional” framing seems too strong**, but I also wouldn’t make current-paradigm AGI the default bet. My best estimate remains **30%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

I’m using a **practical AGI** definition: a system that can do **most economically valuable cognitive work at about human level with modest oversight**. Interpreting the **current paradigm** broadly as continued development of **foundation-model/LLM systems**—more scale, multimodality, better post-training, tool use, retrieval, memory, and more inference-time search—my best estimate, based on evidence through mid-2024, is **30%**.

Why that’s not lower:
- Scaling has been the most reliable empirical driver of AI progress.
- The current paradigm already shows unusually broad transfer across language, code, vision, and reasoning-like tasks.
- Most frontier compute, money, and talent are concentrated on this path.
- A 2035 horizon favors the incumbent: radically new paradigms usually take time to prove out and industrialize.

Why that’s not higher:
- Current systems are still weak on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounded world models, continual learning, and robust autonomy.
- Internet-scale prediction may hit data, compute, or efficiency ceilings.
- Some of the missing pieces for AGI may require more than “just bigger and better LLMs.”
- The line between “refinement” and “paradigm shift” is blurry; some crucial future advances may look incremental at first but be conceptually deeper than they seem.

So: Marcus’s skepticism is understandable, but **“delusional” is too strong**. Continued scaling and refinement of the LLM/foundation-model paradigm is a **serious contender** for reaching AGI before 2035, but not the outcome I’d currently bet on as the most likely.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%**

Using a practical AGI definition—something that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive work at around skilled-human level—I’d put the chance at **about 35%** that it is reached **before 2035 primarily by scaling and refining the current LLM/transformer lineage**, rather than by a fundamentally new paradigm.

Why that high:
- Scaling has already produced broader, more general capabilities than many expected.
- The world’s chips, talent, capital, and tooling are overwhelmingly pointed at this paradigm.
- A lot of the likely “missing pieces” still look like refinements, not replacements: multimodality, better post-training, tool use, retrieval, persistent memory, search/planning, verifiers, synthetic data, and RL on top of LLM cores.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on reliability, grounding, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, and online learning.
- Data, compute, and inference-cost bottlenecks could matter.
- “AGI by 2035” itself is far from certain, even if progress remains fast.

So I think Marcus’s dismissal is **too strong**: this route is **very plausible**, but still **well short of a coin flip**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 40%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “a system that can autonomously do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at around competent-human level,” I’d put the **unconditional probability at about 40%** that it is reached **before 2035 primarily by scaling and refining today’s LLM/foundation-model paradigm**, rather than by a clean break to a fundamentally different approach.

Why it’s not lower:
- Scaling and refinement have already taken transformers much farther than many skeptics expected.
- A lot of what may still be missing—multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, planning/search, RL fine-tuning, more inference-time compute—can plausibly be added **without abandoning the core paradigm**.
- Most of the world’s talent, compute, and capital are concentrated on this path.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems still show real weaknesses in robustness, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, grounding, and reliable autonomous action.
- Data and compute may deliver diminishing returns.
- AI has often needed important conceptual shifts when a dominant approach started to plateau.

So: Marcus’s skepticism is understandable, but “delusional” is too strong. An **LLM-centric route to AGI by 2035 is a serious possibility, but still clearly below even odds**.

rows.answer:
Best estimate: **35%**.

Why not lower:
- Scaling plus refinements has kept producing real capability gains: better data, post-training, tool use, longer context, multimodality, and more test-time compute.
- Most talent, compute, and capital are still concentrated on transformer/foundation-model stacks.
- If AGI arrives before 2035, it’s more likely to come from extending the leading paradigm than from inventing and maturing a wholly new one fast enough.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still brittle on robust reasoning, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and reliability.
- Data/compute bottlenecks and diminishing returns may matter.
- The final gap to AGI may require a new objective or architecture large enough to count as a paradigm shift.

That 35% assumes a functional/economic notion of AGI and counts multimodal, tool-using transformer systems as part of the “LLM path.” So: **35%**—not delusional, but clearly below even odds.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

That’s under a practical AGI definition: a system that can do most economically valuable cognitive tasks at least at a competent human level, with limited supervision.

Why not lower:
- The current LLM/foundation-model line has already produced more general capability than most skeptics expected.
- Most of the money, talent, compute, and organizational momentum are concentrated on scaling and extending this paradigm.
- Many “missing pieces” for AGI—multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, verifier loops, test-time search—look more like extensions of the current stack than wholly new science.
- A 2035 horizon is short enough that incumbency and path dependence matter a lot.

Why not higher:
- Today’s systems still look brittle on reliability, long-horizon planning, grounding, and continual learning.
- Data, power, and diminishing-return constraints could bite.
- It’s quite possible that true AGI needs a more substantial shift toward persistent world models, stronger agency/planning, or new learning paradigms.
- The line between “refinement” and “new paradigm” is fuzzy; some crucial breakthrough may arrive that no longer feels mainly like LLM scaling.

So: I would not call the LLM-to-AGI path “delusional,” but I also would not make it the favorite. My single-number forecast is **35%**.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 35%.**

Why that high:
- The current transformer/LLM paradigm has a strong empirical track record of overcoming alleged ceilings via **scale, better data, RL/post-training, multimodality, tool use, retrieval, and test-time compute**.
- A lot of what people call “AGI” may not require human-like cognition internally; it may be enough to reach **human-level performance on most economically valuable cognitive tasks**, and LLM-centric systems look increasingly plausible for that.
- The world is investing overwhelmingly in this path, so it gets the most talent, compute, and iteration. That path dependence matters.

Why not higher:
- Current systems are still notably weak at **long-horizon autonomy, reliability, grounded world models, causal reasoning, persistent memory, and robust action under distribution shift**.
- Some of those gaps may be fixable with refinement; others may reflect a real architectural limitation.
- **Data quality, compute cost, and diminishing returns** are serious constraints, and it remains unclear whether “more of the same, plus scaffolding” gets all the way to AGI.
- If AGI requires deeper advances in agency, online learning, or embodiment, a more fundamental paradigm shift may be needed.

So my bottom line is:
- **Marcus is probably too categorical** to dismiss the current paradigm outright.
- But it is also **not the base-case favorite** that straightforward scaling/refinement of LLM-centric systems alone gets to AGI by 2035.

**Single-number forecast: 35%.**

For the full proposition as stated, my best estimate is **25%**.

I’m interpreting “scaling and refining LLM architectures” broadly: transformer-style foundation models plus multimodality, retrieval, memory, tool use, agent scaffolding, RL/post-training, and more inference-time compute.

- Scaling has already produced much broader competence than many skeptics expected.
- The current paradigm has strong empirical momentum and huge capital/talent behind it.
- If AGI arrives that early, path dependence makes an evolved foundation-model stack a plausible winner.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current systems still look weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, continual learning, and reliable autonomy.
- There may be data, compute, and energy bottlenecks.
- It’s quite plausible that crossing from “very capable assistant” to AGI requires a real conceptual shift, not just more of the same.

So: Marcus is too categorical, but “AGI mainly from scaled/refined LLMs before 2035” still looks to me like roughly a **one-in-four** event.

rows.answer:
**Best estimate: 30%.**

Assuming **AGI** means something like “able to do most economically valuable cognitive work at roughly skilled-human level,” and assuming the **current paradigm** includes not just bigger transformers but also the now-standard extensions around them—multimodality, tool use, retrieval, memory, better post-training, and more test-time compute—I’d put the chance of AGI arriving **primarily via that route before 2035** at about **30%**.

Why it’s not lower:
- Scaling and refinement have already produced broader capabilities than many skeptics expected.
- Most of the world’s AI talent, compute, capital, and deployment infrastructure are concentrated on the foundation-model path.
- If AGI comes soon, it is often more likely to come from the approach already compounding progress than from a wholly new paradigm that still has to be invented, validated, and industrialized.

Why it’s not higher:
- Current models still look weak on robustness, long-horizon planning, causal/world modeling, grounding, memory, and reliable autonomous action.
- Scaling may keep improving average performance without solving the tail-end reliability and generalization problems AGI would require.
- Data, compute, energy, and economic constraints could slow progress.
- It remains plausible that a real architectural break—not just more scale and engineering—will be needed.

So: I think Marcus’s “delusional” framing is too strong, but I also would **not** make “current LLM paradigm reaches AGI by 2035” the most likely outcome. **30%** is my single best estimate.
